Given that every expression in Lean has a type, it is natural to ask: what type does `Type` itself have?
``Type : Type 1`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Type`
```
Type : Type 1
```
``
You have actually come up against one of the most subtle aspects of Lean's typing system. Lean's underlying foundation has an infinite hierarchy of types:
``Type : Type 1`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Type`
```
Type : Type 1
```
``Type 1 : Type 2`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Type 1`
```
Type 1 : Type 2
```
``Type 2 : Type 3`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Type 2`
```
Type 2 : Type 3
```
``Type 3 : Type 4`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Type 3`
```
Type 3 : Type 4
```
``Type 4 : Type 5`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Type 4`
```
Type 4 : Type 5
```
``
Think of `Type 0` as a universe of “small” or “ordinary” types. `Type 1` is then a larger universe of types, which contains `Type 0` as an element, and `Type 2` is an even larger universe of types, which contains `Type 1` as an element. The list is infinite: there is a `Type n` for every natural number `n`. `Type` is an abbreviation for `Type 0`:
``Type : Type 1`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Type`
```
Type : Type 1
```
``Type : Type 1`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Type 0`
```
Type : Type 1
```
``
The following table may help concretize the relationships being discussed. Movement along the x-axis represents a change in the universe, while movement along the y-axis represents a change in what is sometimes referred to as “degree”.  
|  sort  |  `Prop` (`Sort 0`)  |  `Type` (`Sort 1`)  |  `Type 1` (`Sort 2`)  |  `Type 2` (`Sort 3`)  |  ...  |  
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |  
|  type  |  `[True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference")`  |  `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`  |  `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") -> Type`  |  `Type -> Type 1`  |  ...  |  
|  term  |  `[True.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True.intro in Lean Language Reference")`  |  `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`  |  `fun n => [Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") n`  |  `fun (_ : Type) => Type`  |  ...  |  
Some operations, however, need to be _polymorphic_ over type universes. For example, `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α` should make sense for any type `α`, no matter which type universe `α` lives in. This explains the type signature of the function `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`:
``[List.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type u) : Type u`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") List`
```
[List.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type u) : Type u
```
``
Here `u` is a variable ranging over type levels. The output of the `#check` command means that whenever `α` has type `Type u`, `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α` also has type `Type u`. The function `[Prod](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference")` is similarly polymorphic:
``[Prod.{u, v}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type u) (β : Type v) : Type (max u v)`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Prod`
```
[Prod.{u, v}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type u) (β : Type v) : Type (max u v)
```
``
To define polymorphic constants, Lean allows you to declare universe variables explicitly using the `universe` command:
`[universe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Universes/#Lean___Parser___Command___universe-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") u  ``def F (α : Type u) : Type u := [Prod](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference") α α  ```F.{u} (α : Type u) : Type u`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") F`
```
F.{u} (α : Type u) : Type u
```
``
You can avoid the `universe` command by providing the universe parameters when defining `F`:
`def F.{u} (α : Type u) : Type u := [Prod](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference") α α  ```F.{u} (α : Type u) : Type u`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") F`
```
F.{u} (α : Type u) : Type u
```
``
##  2.3. Function Abstraction and Evaluation[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=function-abstraction-and-evaluation "Permalink")
Lean provides a `fun` (or `λ`) keyword to create a function from an expression as follows:
``fun x => x [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") fun (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) => x + 5`

Creating a function from another expression is a process known as _lambda abstraction_. Suppose you have the variable `x : α` and you can construct an expression `t : β`, then the expression `fun (x : α) => t`, or, equivalently, `λ (x : α) => t`, is an object of type `α → β`. Think of this as the function from `α` to `β` which maps any value `x` to the value `t`.
Here are some more examples

Lean interprets the final three examples as the same expression; in the last expression, Lean infers the type of `x` and `y` from the expression `[if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___not "Documentation for Bool.not in Lean Language Reference") y [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x + 1 [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x + 2`.

Think about what these expressions mean. The expression `fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => x` denotes the identity function on `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, the expression `fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")` denotes the constant function that always returns `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`, and `fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => g (f x)` denotes the composition of `f` and `g`. You can, in general, leave off the type annotation and let Lean infer it for you. So, for example, you can write `fun x => g (f x)` instead of `fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => g (f x)`.
You can pass functions as parameters and by giving them names `f` and `g` you can then use those functions in the implementation:
``fun g f x => g (f x) : ([String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) → ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") fun (g : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) (f : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) => g (f x)`
```
fun g f x => g (f x) : ([String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) → ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")
```
``
You can also pass types as parameters:
``fun α β γ g f x => g (f x) : (α β γ : Type) → (β → γ) → (α → β) → α → γ`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") fun (α β γ : Type) (g : β → γ) (f : α → β) (x : α) => g (f x)`
```
fun α β γ g f x => g (f x) : (α β γ : Type) → (β → γ) → (α → β) → α → γ
```
``
The last expression, for example, denotes the function that takes three types, `α`, `β`, and `γ`, and two functions, `g : β → γ` and `f : α → β`, and returns the composition of `g` and `f`. (Making sense of the type of this function requires an understanding of _dependent products_ , which will be explained below.)
The general form of a lambda expression is `fun (x : α) => t`, where the variable `x` is a “bound variable”: it is really a placeholder, whose “scope” does not extend beyond the expression `t`. For example, the variable `b` in the expression `fun (b : β) (x : α) => b` has nothing to do with the constant `b` declared earlier. In fact, the expression denotes the same function as `fun (u : β) (z : α) => u`.
Formally, expressions that are the same up to a renaming of bound variables are called _alpha equivalent_ , and are considered “the same.” Lean recognizes this equivalence.
Notice that applying a term `t : α → β` to a term `s : α` yields an expression `t s : β`. Returning to the previous example and renaming bound variables for clarity, notice the types of the following expressions:
``(fun x => x) 1 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => x) 1`
```
(fun x => x) 1 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``(fun x => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")) 1 : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (fun `unused variable `x`  Note: This linter can be disabled with `set_option linter.unusedVariables false``x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")) 1`
```
(fun x => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")) 1 : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")
```
` ``def f (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") := toString n ``def g (s : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") := s.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___length "Documentation for String.length in Lean Language Reference") > 0  ```(fun α β γ u v x => u (v x)) [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (fun (α β γ : Type) (u : β → γ) (v : α → β) (x : α) => u (v x)) [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") g f 0`
```
(fun α β γ u v x => u (v x)) [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") g f 0 : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")

``
As expected, the expression `(fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") =>  x) 1` has type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. In fact, more should be true: applying the expression `(fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => x)` to `1` should “return” the value `1`. And, indeed, it does:
``1`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => x) 1`
```
1
```
``true`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (fun `unused variable `x`  Note: This linter can be disabled with `set_option linter.unusedVariables false``x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")) 1`
```
true
```
``
You will see later how these terms are evaluated. For now, notice that this is an important feature of dependent type theory: every term has a computational behavior, and supports a notion of _normalization_. In principle, two terms that reduce to the same value are called _definitionally equal_. They are considered “the same” by Lean's type checker, and Lean does its best to recognize and support these identifications.
Lean is a complete programming language. It has a compiler that generates a binary executable and an interactive interpreter. You can use the command `#eval` to execute expressions, and it is the preferred way of testing your functions.
##  2.4. Definitions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=definitions "Permalink")
Recall that the `def` keyword provides one important way of declaring new named objects.
`def double (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   x + x ```
This might look more familiar to you if you know how functions work in other programming languages. The name `double` is defined as a function that takes an input parameter `x` of type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, where the result of the call is `x + x`, so it is returning type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. You can then invoke this function using:
`def double (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=  x + x `
``6`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double 3`
```
6
```
``
In this case you can think of `def` as a kind of named `fun`. The following yields the same result:
`def double : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   fun x => x + x  ```6`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double 3`
```
6
```
``
You can omit the type declarations when Lean has enough information to infer it. Type inference is an important part of Lean:
`def double :=   fun (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) => x + x ```
The general form of a definition is `def foo : α := bar` where `α` is the type returned from the expression `bar`. Lean can usually infer the type `α`, but it is often a good idea to write it explicitly. This clarifies your intention, and Lean will flag an error if the right-hand side of the definition does not have a matching type.
The right hand side `bar` can be any expression, not just a lambda. So `def` can also be used to simply name a value like this:
`def pi := 3.141592654 ```
`def` can take multiple input parameters. Let's create one that adds two natural numbers:
`def add (x y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) :=   x + y  ```5`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") add 3 2`
```
5
```
``
The parameter list can be separated like this:
`def double (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   x + x `
`def add (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) :=   x + y  ```22`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") add (double 3) (7 + 9)`
```
22
```
``
Notice here we called the `double` function to create the first parameter to `add`.
You can use other more interesting expressions inside a `def`:
`def greater (x y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x > y [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") y ```
You can probably guess what this one will do.
You can also define a function that takes another function as input. The following calls a given function twice passing the output of the first invocation to the second:
`def double (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=  x + x `
`def doTwice (f : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   f (f x)  ```8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") doTwice double 2`
```
8
```
``
Now to get a bit more abstract, you can also specify arguments that are like type parameters:
`def compose (α β γ : Type) (g : β → γ) (f : α → β) (x : α) : γ :=   g (f x) ```
This means `compose` is a function that takes any two functions as input arguments, so long as those functions each take only one input. The type algebra `β → γ` and `α → β` means it is a requirement that the type of the output of the second function must match the type of the input to the first function—which makes sense, otherwise the two functions would not be composable.
`compose` also takes a 3rd argument of type `α` which it uses to invoke the second function (locally named `f`) and it passes the result of that function (which is type `β`) as input to the first function (locally named `g`). The first function returns a type `γ` so that is also the return type of the `compose` function.
`compose` is also very general in that it works over any type `α β γ`. This means `compose` can compose just about any 2 functions so long as they each take one parameter, and so long as the type of output of the second matches the input of the first. For example:
`def compose (α β γ : Type) (g : β → γ) (f : α → β) (x : α) : γ :=   g (f x) def double (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   x + x `
`def square (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   x * x  ```18`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") compose [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") double square 3`
```
18
```
``
##  2.5. Local Definitions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=local-definitions "Permalink")
Lean also allows you to introduce “local” definitions using the `let` keyword. The expression `let a := t1; t2` is definitionally equal to the result of replacing every occurrence of `a` in `t2` by `t1`.
``let y := 2 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 2; y [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") let y := 2 + 2; y * y`
```
let y := 2 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 2;
y [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``16`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") let y := 2 + 2; y * y`
```
16
```
` ``def twice_double (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   let y := x + x; y * y  ```16`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [twice_double](Dependent-Type-Theory/#twice_double "Definition of example") 2`
```
16
```
``
Here, `[twice_double](Dependent-Type-Theory/#twice_double "Definition of example") x` is definitionally equal to the term `(x + x) * (x + x)`.
You can combine multiple assignments by chaining `let` statements:
``let y := 2 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 2; let z := y [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") y; z [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") z : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") let y := 2 + 2; let z := y + y; z * z`
```
let y := 2 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 2;
let z := y [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") y;
z [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") z : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``64`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") let y := 2 + 2; let z := y + y; z * z`
```
64
```
``
The `;` can be omitted when a line break is used.
`def t (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   let y := x + x   y * y ```
Notice that the meaning of the expression `let a := t1; t2` is very similar to the meaning of `(fun a => t2) t1`, but the two are not the same. In the first expression, you should think of every instance of `a` in `t2` as a syntactic abbreviation for `t1`. In the second expression, `a` is a variable, and the expression `fun a => t2` has to make sense independently of the value of `a`. The `let` construct is a stronger means of abbreviation, and there are expressions of the form `let a := t1; t2` that cannot be expressed as `(fun a => t2) t1`. As an exercise, try to understand why the definition of `foo` below type checks, but the definition of `bar` does not.
`def foo := let a := [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"); fun x : a => x + 2 ``/-   def bar := (fun a => fun x : a => x + 2) Nat -/ `
##  2.6. Variables and Sections[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=variables-and-sections "Permalink")
Consider the following three function definitions:
`def compose (α β γ : Type) (g : β → γ) (f : α → β) (x : α) : γ :=   g (f x)  ``def doTwice (α : Type) (h : α → α) (x : α) : α :=   h (h x)  ``def doThrice (α : Type) (h : α → α) (x : α) : α :=   h (h (h x)) ```
Lean provides you with the `variable` command to make such declarations look more compact:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α β γ : Type)  ``def compose (g : β → γ) (f : α → β) (x : α) : γ :=   g (f x)  ``def doTwice (h : α → α) (x : α) : α :=   h (h x)  ``def doThrice (h : α → α) (x : α) : α :=   h (h (h x)) ```
You can declare variables of any type, not just `Type` itself:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α β γ : Type) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (g : β → γ) (f : α → β) (h : α → α) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (x : α)  ``def compose := g (f x) ``def doTwice := h (h x) ``def doThrice := h (h (h x))  ```def compose : (α β γ : Type) → (β → γ) → (α → β) → α → γ := fun α β γ g f x => g (f x)`#print compose`
```
def compose : (α β γ : Type) → (β → γ) → (α → β) → α → γ :=
fun α β γ g f x => g (f x)
```
``def doTwice : (α : Type) → (α → α) → α → α := fun α h x => h (h x)`#print doTwice`
```
def doTwice : (α : Type) → (α → α) → α → α :=
fun α h x => h (h x)
```
``def doThrice : (α : Type) → (α → α) → α → α := fun α h x => h (h (h x))`#print doThrice`
```
def doThrice : (α : Type) → (α → α) → α → α :=
fun α h x => h (h (h x))
```
``
Printing them out shows that all three groups of definitions have exactly the same effect.
The `variable` command instructs Lean to insert the declared variables as bound variables in definitions that refer to them by name. Lean is smart enough to figure out which variables are used explicitly or implicitly in a definition. You can therefore proceed as though `α`, `β`, `γ`, `g`, `f`, `h`, and `x` are fixed objects when you write your definitions, and let Lean abstract the definitions for you automatically.
When declared in this way, a variable stays in scope until the end of the file you are working on. Sometimes, however, it is useful to limit the scope of a variable. For that purpose, Lean provides the notion of a `section`:
`[section](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___section "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") useful ``  [variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α β γ : Type) ``  [variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (g : β → γ) (f : α → β) (h : α → α) ``  [variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (x : α)  ``  def compose := g (f x) ``  def doTwice := h (h x) ``  def doThrice := h (h (h x)) ``end useful ```
When the section is closed, the variables go out of scope, and cannot be referenced any more.
You do not have to indent the lines within a section. Nor do you have to name a section, which is to say, you can use an anonymous `section` / `end` pair. If you do name a section, however, you have to close it using the same name. Sections can also be nested, which allows you to declare new variables incrementally.
##  2.7. Namespaces[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=namespaces "Permalink")
Lean provides you with the ability to group definitions into nested, hierarchical _namespaces_ :
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Foo ``  def a : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := 5 ``  def f (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := x + 7  ``  def fa : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := f a ``  def ffa : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := f (f a)  ``  `Foo.a : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") a`

When you declare that you are working in the namespace `Foo`, every identifier you declare has a full name with prefix “`Foo.`”. Within the namespace, you can refer to identifiers by their shorter names, but once you end the namespace, you have to use the longer names. Unlike `section`, namespaces require a name. There is only one anonymous namespace at the root level.
The `[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` command brings the shorter names into the current context. Often, when you import a module, you will want to open one or more of the namespaces it contains, to have access to the short identifiers. But sometimes you will want to leave this information protected by a fully qualified name, for example, when they conflict with identifiers in another namespace you want to use. Thus namespaces give you a way to manage names in your working environment.
For example, Lean groups definitions and theorems involving lists into a namespace `List`.
``[List.nil.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") {α : Type u} : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [List.nil](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")`
```

``[List.cons.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") {α : Type u} (head : α) (tail : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [List.cons](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")`

Like sections, nested namespaces have to be closed in the order they are opened. Namespaces and sections serve different purposes: namespaces organize data and sections declare variables for insertion in definitions. Sections are also useful for delimiting the scope of commands such as `set_option` and `open`.
In many respects, however, a `namespace`` ... ``end` block behaves the same as a `section`` ... ``end` block. In particular, if you use the `variable` command within a namespace, its scope is limited to the namespace. Similarly, if you use an `open` command within a namespace, its effects disappear when the namespace is closed.
##  2.8. What makes dependent type theory dependent?[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=what-makes-dependent-type-theory-dependent "Permalink")
The short explanation is that types can depend on parameters. You have already seen a nice example of this: the type `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α` depends on the argument `α`, and this dependence is what distinguishes `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` and `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`. For another example, consider the type `Vector α n`, the type of vectors of elements of `α` of length `n`. This type depends on _two_ parameters: the type of the elements in the vector (`α : Type`) and the length of the vector `n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`.
Suppose you wish to write a function `cons` which inserts a new element at the head of a list. What type should `cons` have? Such a function is _polymorphic_ : you expect the `cons` function for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`, or an arbitrary type `α` to behave the same way. So it makes sense to take the type to be the first argument to `cons`, so that for any type, `α`, `[cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#cons "Definition of example") α` is the insertion function for lists of type `α`. In other words, for every `α`, `[cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#cons "Definition of example") α` is the function that takes an element `a : α` and a list `as : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`, and returns a new list, so you have `[cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#cons "Definition of example") α a as : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`.
It is clear that `[cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#cons "Definition of example") α` should have type `α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`. But what type should `[cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#cons "Definition of example")` have? A first guess might be `Type → α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`, but, on reflection, this does not make sense: the `α` in this expression does not refer to anything, whereas it should refer to the argument of type `Type`. In other words, _assuming_ `α : Type` is the first argument to the function, the type of the next two elements are `α` and `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`. These types vary depending on the first argument, `α`.
`def cons (α : Type) (a : α) (as : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α :=   [List.cons](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") a as  ```[cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#cons "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#cons "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`

This is an instance of a _dependent function type_ , or **dependent arrow type**. Given `α : Type` and `β : α → Type`, think of `β` as a family of types over `α`, that is, a type `β a` for each `a : α`. In that case, the type `(a : α) → β a` denotes the type of functions `f` with the property that, for each `a : α`, `f a` is an element of `β a`. In other words, the type of the value returned by `f` depends on its input.
Notice that `(a : α) → β` makes sense for any expression `β : Type`. When the value of `β` depends on `a` (as does, for example, the expression `β a` in the previous paragraph), `(a : α) → β` denotes a dependent function type. When `β` doesn't depend on `a`, `(a : α) → β` is no different from the type `α → β`. Indeed, in dependent type theory (and in Lean), `α → β` is just notation for `(a : α) → β` when `β` does not depend on `a`.
Returning to the example of lists, you can use the command `#check` to inspect the type of the following `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")` functions. The `@` symbol and the difference between the round and curly braces will be explained momentarily.

Just as dependent function types `(a : α) → β a` generalize the notion of a function type `α → β` by allowing `β` to depend on `a`, dependent Cartesian product types `(a : α) × β a` generalize the Cartesian product `α × β` in the same way. Dependent products are also called _sigma_ types, and you can also write them as `Σ a : α, β a`. You can use `⟨a, b⟩` or `[Sigma.mk](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Sigma___mk "Documentation for Sigma.mk in Lean Language Reference") a b` to create a dependent pair. The `⟨` and `⟩` characters may be typed with `\langle` and `\rangle` or `\<` and `\>`, respectively.

```

##  2.9. Implicit Arguments[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=implicit-arguments "Permalink")
Suppose we have an implementation of lists as:

``
Then, you can construct lists of `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` as follows:
`[universe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Universes/#Lean___Parser___Command___universe-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") u def Lst (α : Type u) : Type u := [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α def Lst.cons (α : Type u) (a : α) (as : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α) : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α := [List.cons](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") a as def Lst.nil (α : Type u) : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α := [List.nil](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") def Lst.append (α : Type u) (as bs : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α) : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α := [List.append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___append "Documentation for List.append in Lean Language Reference") as bs `
``[Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") 0 ([Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") 0 ([Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
[Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") 0 ([Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")

` ``def as : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := [Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") ``def bs : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := [Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") 5 ([Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))  ```[Lst.append](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") as bs : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Lst.append](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") as bs`

``
Because the constructors are polymorphic over types, we have to insert the type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` as an argument repeatedly. But this information is redundant: one can infer the argument `α` in `[Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") 5 ([Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))` from the fact that the second argument, `5`, has type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. One can similarly infer the argument in `[Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, not from anything else in that expression, but from the fact that it is sent as an argument to the function `[Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example")`, which expects an element of type `[Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α` in that position.
This is a central feature of dependent type theory: terms carry a lot of information, and often some of that information can be inferred from the context. In Lean, one uses an underscore, `_`, to specify that the system should fill in the information automatically. This is known as an “implicit argument.”

It is still tedious, however, to type all these underscores. When a function takes an argument that can generally be inferred from context, Lean allows you to specify that this argument should, by default, be left implicit. This is done by putting the arguments in curly braces, as follows:
`[universe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Universes/#Lean___Parser___Command___universe-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") u ``def Lst (α : Type u) : Type u := [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α  ``def Lst.cons {α : Type u} (a : α) (as : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α) : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α := [List.cons](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") a as ``def Lst.nil {α : Type u} : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α := [List.nil](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") ``def Lst.append {α : Type u} (as bs : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α) : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") α := [List.append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___append "Documentation for List.append in Lean Language Reference") as bs  ```[Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") 0 [Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") 0 [Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example")`
```
[Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") 0 [Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
` ``def as : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := [Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") ``def bs : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := [Lst.cons](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") 5 [Lst.nil](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example")  ```as.[append](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") bs : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Lst.append](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") as bs`
```
as.[append](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") bs : [Lst](Dependent-Type-Theory/#Lst "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")

``
All that has changed are the braces around `α : Type u` in the declaration of the variables. We can also use this device in function definitions:
`[universe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Universes/#Lean___Parser___Command___universe-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") u ``def ident {α : Type u} (x : α) := x ```
Checking the type of `ident` requires wrapping it in parentheses to avoid having its signature shown:
`[universe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Universes/#Lean___Parser___Command___universe-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") u def ident {α : Type u} (x : α) := x `
``ident : ?m.1 → ?m.1`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (ident)`
```
ident : ?m.1 → ?m.1
```
``ident 1 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ident 1`
```
ident 1 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``ident "hello" : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ident "hello"`
```
ident "hello" : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")
```
``@ident : {α : Type u_1} → α → α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @ident`
```
@ident : {α : Type u_1} → α → α
```
``
The makes the first argument to `ident` implicit. Notationally, this hides the specification of the type, making it look as though `ident` simply takes an argument of any type. In fact, the function `id` is defined in the standard library in exactly this way. We have chosen a nontraditional name here only to avoid a clash of names.
Variables can also be specified as implicit when they are declared with the `variable` command:
`[universe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Universes/#Lean___Parser___Command___universe-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") u  ``[section](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___section "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ``  [variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") {α : Type u} ``  [variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (x : α) ``  def ident := x ``end  ```ident.{u} {α : Type u} (x : α) : α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ident`
```
ident.{u} {α : Type u} (x : α) : α
```
``ident 4 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ident 4`
```
ident 4 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``ident "hello" : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ident "hello"`
```
ident "hello" : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")
```
``
This definition of `ident` here has the same effect as the one above.
Lean has very complex mechanisms for instantiating implicit arguments, and we will see that they can be used to infer function types, predicates, and even proofs. The process of instantiating these “holes,” or “placeholders,” in a term is often known as _elaboration_. The presence of implicit arguments means that at times there may be insufficient information to fix the meaning of an expression precisely. An expression like `id` or `[List.nil](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")` is said to be _polymorphic_ , because it can take on different meanings in different contexts.
One can always specify the type `T` of an expression `e` by writing `(e : T)`. This instructs Lean's elaborator to use the value `T` as the type of `e` when trying to resolve implicit arguments. In the second pair of examples below, this mechanism is used to specify the desired types of the expressions `id` and `[List.nil](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")`:
``[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ?m.1`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([List.nil](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ?m.1
```
``id : ?m.1 → ?m.1`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (id)`
```
id : ?m.1 → ?m.1
```
` ```[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([List.nil](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`

```
id : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")

``
Numerals are overloaded in Lean, but when the type of a numeral cannot be inferred, Lean assumes, by default, that it is a natural number. So the expressions in the first two `#check` commands below are elaborated in the same way, whereas the third `#check` command interprets `2` as an integer.
``2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") 2`
```

```

```
2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``2 : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (2 : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference"))`
```

```
``
Sometimes, however, we may find ourselves in a situation where we have declared an argument to a function to be implicit, but now want to provide the argument explicitly. If `foo` is such a function, the notation `@foo` denotes the same function with all the arguments made explicit.
``@id : {α : Sort u_1} → α → α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @id`

```

` ```id 1 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @id [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") 1`

``
Notice that now the first `#check` command gives the type of the identifier, `id`, without inserting any placeholders. Moreover, the output indicates that the first argument is implicit.
