[Tactics for Inductive Types](Inductive-Types/#tactics-for-inductive-types) below will introduce additional tactics that are specifically designed to make use of inductive types.
Notice that, under the [propositions-as-types](Propositions-and-Proofs/#--tech-term-propositions-as-types) correspondence, we can use `match` to prove theorems as well as define functions. In other words, under the [propositions-as-types](Propositions-and-Proofs/#--tech-term-propositions-as-types) correspondence, the proof by cases is a kind of definition by cases, where what is being “defined” is a proof instead of a piece of data.
The `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` type in the Lean library is an instance of enumerated type.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Bool where   | false : [Bool](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Bool "Definition of example")   | true  : [Bool](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Bool "Definition of example") `
`end Hidden `
(To run these examples, we put them in a namespace called `Hidden`, so that a name like `Bool` does not conflict with the `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` in the standard library. This is necessary because these types are part of the Lean “prelude” that is automatically imported when the system is started.)
As an exercise, you should think about what the introduction and elimination rules for these types do. As a further exercise, we suggest defining boolean operations `[and](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___and "Documentation for Bool.and in Lean Language Reference")`, `[or](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___or "Documentation for Bool.or in Lean Language Reference")`, `[not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___not "Documentation for Bool.not in Lean Language Reference")` on the `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` type, and verifying common identities. Note that you can define a binary operation like `[and](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___and "Documentation for Bool.and in Lean Language Reference")` using `match`:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`def and (a b : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") a [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")  => b   | [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference") => [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference") `
`end Hidden `
Similarly, most identities can be proved by introducing suitable `match`, and then using `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")`.
##  7.2. Constructors with Arguments[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=constructors-with-arguments "Permalink")
Enumerated types are a very special case of inductive types, in which the constructors take no arguments at all. In general, a “construction” can depend on data, which is then represented in the constructed argument. Consider the definitions of the product type and sum type in the library:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Prod (α : Type u) (β : Type v)   | mk : α → β → Prod α β  ``inductive Sum (α : Type u) (β : Type v) where   | inl : α → Sum α β   | inr : β → Sum α β `
`end Hidden `
Consider what is going on in these examples. The product type has one constructor, `[Prod.mk](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference")`, which takes two arguments. To define a function on `Prod α β`, we can assume the input is of the form `[Prod.mk](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference") a b`, and we have to specify the output, in terms of `a` and `b`. We can use this to define the two projections for `Prod`. Remember that the standard library defines notation `α × β` for `Prod α β` and `(a, b)` for `[Prod.mk](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference") a b`.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden inductive Prod (α : Type u) (β : Type v)   | mk : α → β → Prod α β `
`def fst {α : Type u} {β : Type v} (p : Prod α β) : α :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | Prod.mk a `unused variable `b`  Note: This linter can be disabled with `set_option linter.unusedVariables false``b => a ``def snd {α : Type u} {β : Type v} (p : Prod α β) : β :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | Prod.mk `unused variable `a`  Note: This linter can be disabled with `set_option linter.unusedVariables false``a b => b `
`end Hidden `
The function `fst` takes a pair, `p`. The `match` interprets `p` as a pair, `Prod.mk a b`. Recall also from [Dependent Type Theory](Dependent-Type-Theory/#dependent-type-theory) that to give these definitions the greatest generality possible, we allow the types `α` and `β` to belong to any universe.
Here is another example where we use the recursor `Prod.casesOn` instead of `match`.
`def prod_example (p : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") × [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   Prod.casesOn (motive := fun _ => [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) p     (fun b n => [cond](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#cond "Documentation for cond in Lean Language Reference") b (2 * n) (2 * n + 1))  ```6`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [prod_example](Inductive-Types/#prod_example "Definition of example") ([true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference"), 3)`
```
6
```
` ```7`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [prod_example](Inductive-Types/#prod_example "Definition of example") ([false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference"), 3)`
```
7
```
``
The argument `motive` is used to specify the type of the object you want to construct, and it is a function because it may depend on the pair. The `[cond](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#cond "Documentation for cond in Lean Language Reference")` function is a boolean conditional: `[cond](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#cond "Documentation for cond in Lean Language Reference") b t1 t2` returns `t1` if `b` is true, and `t2` otherwise. The function `prod_example` takes a pair consisting of a boolean, `b`, and a number, `n`, and returns either `2 * n` or `2 * n + 1` according to whether `b` is true or false.
In contrast, the sum type has _two_ constructors, `[inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference")` and `[inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference")` (for “insert left” and “insert right”), each of which takes _one_ (explicit) argument. To define a function on `[Sum](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum in Lean Language Reference") α β`, we have to handle two cases: either the input is of the form `[inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") a`, in which case we have to specify an output value in terms of `a`, or the input is of the form `[inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") b`, in which case we have to specify an output value in terms of `b`.
`def sum_example (s : [Sum](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   Sum.casesOn (motive := fun _ => [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) s     (fun n => 2 * n)     (fun n => 2 * n + 1)  ```6`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [sum_example](Inductive-Types/#sum_example "Definition of example") ([Sum.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") 3)`
```
6
```
` ```7`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [sum_example](Inductive-Types/#sum_example "Definition of example") ([Sum.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") 3)`
```
7
```
``
This example is similar to the previous one, but now an input to `sum_example` is implicitly either of the form `[inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") n` or `[inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") n`. In the first case, the function returns `2 * n`, and the second case, it returns `2 * n + 1`.
Notice that the product type depends on parameters `α β : Type` which are arguments to the constructors as well as `[Prod](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference")`. Lean detects when these arguments can be inferred from later arguments to a constructor or the return type, and makes them implicit in that case.
In [Defining the Natural Numbers](Inductive-Types/#defining-the-natural-numbers) we will see what happens when the constructor of an inductive type takes arguments from the inductive type itself. What characterizes the examples we consider in this section is that each constructor relies only on previously specified types.
Notice that a type with multiple constructors is disjunctive: an element of `[Sum](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum in Lean Language Reference") α β` is either of the form `[inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") a` _or_ of the form `[inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") b`. A constructor with multiple arguments introduces conjunctive information: from an element `[Prod.mk](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference") a b` of `[Prod](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference") α β` we can extract `a` _and_ `b`. An arbitrary inductive type can include both features, by having any number of constructors, each of which takes any number of arguments.
As with function definitions, Lean's inductive definition syntax will let you put named arguments to the constructors before the colon:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Prod (α : Type u) (β : Type v) where   | mk (fst : α) (snd : β) : Prod α β  ``inductive Sum (α : Type u) (β : Type v) where   | inl (a : α) : Sum α β   | inr (b : β) : Sum α β `
`end Hidden `
The results of these definitions are essentially the same as the ones given earlier in this section.
A type, like `Prod`, that has only one constructor is purely conjunctive: the constructor simply packs the list of arguments into a single piece of data, essentially a tuple where the type of subsequent arguments can depend on the type of the initial argument. We can also think of such a type as a “record” or a “structure”. In Lean, the keyword `structure` can be used to define such an inductive type as well as its projections, at the same time.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`structure Prod (α : Type u) (β : Type v) where   mk ::   fst : α   snd : β `
`end Hidden `
This example simultaneously introduces the inductive type, `Prod`, its constructor, `mk`, the usual eliminators (`rec` and `recOn`), as well as the projections, `fst` and `snd`, as defined above.
If you do not name the constructor, Lean uses `mk` as a default. For example, the following defines a record to store a color as a triple of RGB values:
`structure Color where   red : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   green : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   blue : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")  ``def yellow := Color.mk 255 255 0  ```255`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Color.red [yellow](Inductive-Types/#Color "Definition of example")`
```
255
```
``
The definition of `yellow` forms the record with the three values shown, and the projection `Color.red` returns the red component.
The `structure` command is especially useful for defining algebraic structures, and Lean provides substantial infrastructure to support working with them. Here, for example, is the definition of a semigroup:
`structure Semigroup where   carrier : Type u   mul : carrier → carrier → carrier   mul_assoc : ∀ a b c, mul (mul a b) c = mul a (mul b c) ```
We will see more examples in the chapter on [structures and records](Structures-and-Records/#structures-and-records).
We have already discussed the dependent product type `Sigma`:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Sigma {α : Type u} (β : α → Type v) where   | mk : (a : α) → β a → [Sigma](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Sigma "Definition of example") β `
`end Hidden `
Two more examples of inductive types in the library are the following:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Option (α : Type u) where   | none : [Option](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Option "Definition of example") α   | some : α → [Option](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Option "Definition of example") α  ``inductive Inhabited (α : Type u) where   | mk : α → [Inhabited](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Option "Definition of example") α `
`end Hidden `
In the semantics of dependent type theory, there is no built-in notion of a partial function. Every element of a function type `α → β` or a dependent function type `(a : α) → β` is assumed to have a value at every input. The `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` type provides a way of representing partial functions. An element of `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") β` is either `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")` or of the form `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") b`, for some value `b : β`. Thus we can think of an element `f` of the type `α → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") β` as being a partial function from `α` to `β`: for every `a : α`, `f a` either returns `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, indicating `f a` is “undefined”, or `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") b`.
An element of `[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") α` is simply a witness to the fact that there is an element of `α`. Later, we will see that `[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference")` is an example of a _type class_ in Lean: Lean can be instructed that suitable base types are inhabited, and can automatically infer that other constructed types are inhabited on that basis.
As exercises, we encourage you to develop a notion of composition for partial functions from `α` to `β` and `β` to `γ`, and show that it behaves as expected. We also encourage you to show that `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` are inhabited, that the product of two inhabited types is inhabited, and that the type of functions to an inhabited type is inhabited.
##  7.3. Inductively Defined Propositions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=inductively-defined-propositions "Permalink")
Inductively defined types can live in any type universe, including the bottom-most one, `Prop`. In fact, this is exactly how the logical connectives are defined.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive False : Prop  ``inductive True : Prop where   | intro : [True](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___False "Definition of example")  ``inductive And (a b : Prop) : Prop where   | intro : a → b → [And](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___False "Definition of example") a b  ``inductive Or (a b : Prop) : Prop where   | inl : a → [Or](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___False "Definition of example") a b   | inr : b → [Or](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___False "Definition of example") a b `
`end Hidden `
You should think about how these give rise to the introduction and elimination rules that you have already seen. There are rules that govern what the eliminator of an inductive type can eliminate _to_ , that is, what kinds of types can be the target of a recursor. Roughly speaking, what characterizes inductive types in `Prop` is that one can only eliminate to other types in `Prop`. This is consistent with the understanding that if `p : Prop`, an element `hp : p` carries no data. There is a small exception to this rule, however, which we will discuss below, in [Inductive Families](Inductive-Types/#inductive-families).
Even the existential quantifier is inductively defined:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Exists {α : Sort u} (p : α → Prop) : Prop where   | intro (w : α) (h : p w) : [Exists](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Exists "Definition of example") p `
`end Hidden `
Keep in mind that the notation `∃ x : α, p` is syntactic sugar for `[Exists](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists in Lean Language Reference") (fun x : α => p)`.
The definitions of `[False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")`, `[True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference")`, `[And](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference")`, and `[Or](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference")` are perfectly analogous to the definitions of `[Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Prod](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference")`, and `[Sum](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum in Lean Language Reference")`. The difference is that the first group yields elements of `Prop`, and the second yields elements of `Type u` for some `u`. In a similar way, `∃ x : α, p` is a `Prop`-valued variant of `Σ x : α, β`.
This is a good place to mention another inductive type, denoted `{x : α // p}`, which is sort of a hybrid between `∃ x : α, p` and `Σ x : α, β`.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Subtype {α : Type u} (p : α → Prop) where   | mk : (x : α) → p x → Subtype p `
`end Hidden `
In fact, in Lean, `Subtype` is defined using the structure command:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`structure Subtype {α : Sort u} (p : α → Prop) where   val : α   property : p val `
`end Hidden `
The notation `{x : α // p x}` is syntactic sugar for `[Subtype](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Subtypes/#Subtype___mk "Documentation for Subtype in Lean Language Reference") (fun x : α => p x)`. It is modeled after subset notation in set theory: the idea is that `{x : α // p x}` denotes the collection of elements of `α` that have property `p`.
##  7.4. Defining the Natural Numbers[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=defining-the-natural-numbers "Permalink")
The inductively defined types we have seen so far are “flat”: constructors wrap data and insert it into a type, and the corresponding recursor unpacks the data and acts on it. Things get much more interesting when the constructors act on elements of the very type being defined. A canonical example is the type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` of natural numbers:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Nat where   | zero : Nat   | succ : Nat → Nat `
`end Hidden `
There are two constructors. We start with `[zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference") : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`; it takes no arguments, so we have it from the start. In contrast, the constructor `[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference")` can only be applied to a previously constructed `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. Applying it to `[zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")` yields `[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") [zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference") : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. Applying it again yields `[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") ([succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") [zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, and so on. Intuitively, `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is the “smallest” type with these constructors, meaning that it is exhaustively (and freely) generated by starting with `[zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")` and applying `[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference")` repeatedly.
As before, the recursor for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is designed to define a dependent function `f` from `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to any domain, that is, an element `f` of `(n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) → motive n` for some `motive : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Sort u`. It has to handle two cases: the case where the input is `[zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")`, and the case where the input is of the form `[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") n` for some `n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. In the first case, we simply specify a target value with the appropriate type, as before. In the second case, however, the recursor can assume that a value of `f` at `n` has already been computed. As a result, the next argument to the recursor specifies a value for `f ([succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") n)` in terms of `n` and `f n`. If we check the type of the recursor, you find the following:
`Nat.rec.{u} :   {motive : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Sort u} →   (zero : motive [Nat.zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")) →   (succ : (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) → motive n → motive ([Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") n)) →   (t : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) → motive t `
The implicit argument, `motive`, is the codomain of the function being defined. In type theory it is common to say `motive` is the _motive_ for the elimination/recursion, since it describes the kind of object we wish to construct. The next two arguments specify how to compute the zero and successor cases, as described above. They are also known as the _minor premises_. Finally, the `t : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is the input to the function. It is also known as the _major premise_.
The `Nat.recOn` is similar to `Nat.rec` but the major premise occurs before the minor premises.
`Nat.recOn.{u} :   {motive : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Sort u} →   (t : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) →   (zero : motive [Nat.zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")) →   (succ : ((n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) → motive n → motive ([Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") n))) →   motive t `
Consider, for example, the addition function `add m n` on the natural numbers. Fixing `m`, we can define addition by recursion on `n`. In the base case, we set `add m [zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")` to `m`. In the successor step, assuming the value `add m n` is already determined, we define `add m ([succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") n)` to be `[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") (add m n)`.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Nat where   | zero : Nat   | succ : Nat → Nat deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")  ``def add (m n : Nat) : Nat :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") n [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | Nat.zero   => m   | [Nat.succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") n => [Nat.succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") ([add](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") m n)  ``[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Nat  ```Hidden.Nat.succ (Hidden.Nat.succ (Hidden.Nat.succ (Hidden.Nat.zero)))`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [add](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") ([succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") ([succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") zero)) ([succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") zero)`
```
Hidden.Nat.succ (Hidden.Nat.succ (Hidden.Nat.succ (Hidden.Nat.zero)))
```

`end Hidden `
It is useful to put such definitions into a namespace, `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. We can then go on to define familiar notation in that namespace. The two defining equations for addition now hold definitionally:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden inductive Nat where  | zero : Nat  | succ : Nat → Nat deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference") `
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Nat  ``def add (m n : Nat) : Nat :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") n [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | Nat.zero   => m   | [Nat.succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") n => [Nat.succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") ([add](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat___add "Definition of example") m n)  ``instance : [Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference") Nat where   [add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add.add in Lean Language Reference") := [add](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat___add "Definition of example")  ``theorem add_zero (m : Nat) : m + zero = m := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") ``theorem add_succ (m n : Nat) : m + [succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") n = [succ](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Nat-next "Definition of example") (m + n) := rfl  ``end Nat `
`end Hidden `
We will explain how the `instance` command works in the [Type Classes](Type-Classes/#type-classes) chapter. In the examples below, we will use Lean's version of the natural numbers.
Proving a fact like `0 + n = n`, however, requires a proof by induction. As observed above, the induction principle is just a special case of the recursion principle, when the codomain `motive n` is an element of `Prop`. It represents the familiar pattern of an inductive proof: to prove `∀ n, motive n`, first prove `motive 0`, and then, for arbitrary `n`, assume `ih : motive n` and prove `motive (n + 1)`.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Nat  ``theorem zero_add (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : 0 + n = n :=   Nat.recOn (motive := fun x => 0 + x = x)    n    (show 0 + 0 = 0 from [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference"))    (fun (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (ih : 0 + n = n) =>     show 0 + (n + 1) = n + 1 from     calc 0 + (n + 1)       _ = (0 + n) + 1 := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")       _ = n + 1       := byn✝:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") n [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") n `end Hidden `
Notice that, once again, when `Nat.recOn` is used in the context of a proof, it is really the induction principle in disguise. The `rw` and `simp` tactics tend to be very effective in proofs like these. In this case, each can be used to reduce the proof to:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Nat  ``theorem zero_add (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : 0 + n = n :=   Nat.recOn (motive := fun x => 0 + x = x) n     [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")     (fun n ih => byn✝:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")n:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")ih:(fun x => 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x) n⊢ (fun x => 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x) n.[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [`This simp argument is unused:   ih  Hint: Omit it from the simp argument list.   simp ̵[̵i̵h̵]̵  Note: This linter can be disabled with `set_option linter.unusedSimpArgs false``ih]All goals completed! 🐙) `
`end Hidden `
As another example, let us prove the associativity of addition, `∀ m n k, m + n + k = m + (n + k)`. (The notation `+`, as we have defined it, associates to the left, so `m + n + k` is really `(m + n) + k`.) The hardest part is figuring out which variable to do the induction on. Since addition is defined by recursion on the second argument, `k` is a good guess, and once we make that choice the proof almost writes itself:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `

[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : m + n + k = m + (n + k) :=   Nat.recOn (motive := fun k => m + n + k = m + (n + k)) k     (show m + n + 0 = m + (n + 0) from [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean ##  7.5. Other Recursive Data Types[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=other-recursive-data-types "Permalink")
Let us consider some more examples of inductively defined types. For any type, `α`, the type `[List](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___List "Definition of example") α` of lists of elements of `α` is defined in the library.

A list of elements of type `α` is either the empty list, `nil`, or an element `h : α` followed by a list `t : [List](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___List "Definition of example") α`. The first element, `h`, is commonly known as the “head” of the list, and the remainder, `t`, is known as the “tail.”

Try also defining the function `length : {α : Type u} → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` that returns the length of a list, and prove that it behaves as expected (for example, `length ([append](Induction-and-Recursion/#append "Definition of example") as bs) = length as + length bs`).
For another example, we can define the type of binary trees:
`inductive BinaryTree where   | leaf : [BinaryTree](Inductive-Types/#BinaryTree "Definition of example")   | node : [BinaryTree](Inductive-Types/#BinaryTree "Definition of example") → [BinaryTree](Inductive-Types/#BinaryTree "Definition of example") → [BinaryTree](Inductive-Types/#BinaryTree "Definition of example") ```
In fact, we can even define the type of countably branching trees:

##  7.6. Tactics for Inductive Types[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=tactics-for-inductive-types "Permalink")
Given the fundamental importance of inductive types in Lean, it should not be surprising that there are a number of tactics designed to work with them effectively. We describe some of them here.
The `cases` tactic works on elements of an inductively defined type, and does what the name suggests: it decomposes the element according to each of the possible constructors. In its most basic form, it is applied to an element `x` in the local context. It then reduces the goal to cases in which `x` is replaced by each of the constructions.

In the second branch, it is:

There are extra bells and whistles. For one thing, `[cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` allows you to choose the names for each alternative using a `with` clause. In the next example, for example, we choose the name `m` for the argument to `[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference")`, so that the second case refers to `[succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") m`. More importantly, the cases tactic will detect any items in the local context that depend on the target variable. It reverts these elements, does the split, and reintroduces them. In the example below, notice that the hypothesis `h : n ≠ 0` becomes `h : 0 ≠ 0` in the first branch, and `h : m [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 ≠ 0` in the second.

Here is an example of multiple constructors with arguments.
`inductive Foo where   | bar1 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Foo   | bar2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Foo  ``def silly (x : Foo) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := byx:Foo⊢ [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   [cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") x with   | [bar1](Inductive-Types/#Foo "Definition of example") a b =>bar1a:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")b:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") bAll goals completed! The alternatives for each constructor don't need to be solved in the order the constructors were declared.
`inductive Foo where   | bar1 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Foo   | bar2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Foo `

You can also use `[cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` with an arbitrary expression. Assuming that expression occurs in the goal, the cases tactic will generalize over the expression, introduce the resulting universally quantified variable, and case on that.

Notice that the expression `m + 3 * k` is erased by `[generalize](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#generalize "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`; all that matters is whether it is of the form `0` or `n✝ [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1`. This form of `[cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` will _not_ revert any hypotheses that also mention the expression in the equation (in this case, `m + 3 * k`). If such a term appears in a hypothesis and you want to generalize over that as well, you need to `revert` it explicitly.
If the expression you case on does not appear in the goal, the `cases` tactic uses `have` to put the type of the expression into the context. Here is an example:

Here is another example, where we use the decidability of equality on the natural numbers to split on the cases `m = n` and `m ≠ n`.
``Nat.sub_self (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : n [-](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HSub___mk "Documentation for HSub.hSub in Lean Language Reference") n [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Nat.sub_self`

```

Remember that if you `open ``Classical`, you can use the law of the excluded middle for any proposition at all. But using type class inference (see [Type Classes](Type-Classes/#type-classes)), Lean can actually find the relevant decision procedure, which means that you can use the case split in a computable function.
Just as the `[cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic can be used to carry out proof by cases, the `[induction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#induction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic can be used to carry out proofs by induction. The syntax is similar to that of `[cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, except that the argument can only be a term in the local context. Here is an example:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `

🐙   | [succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") n ih =>succn:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")ih:0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") n [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") n⊢ 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") [(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference")n [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") n [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 [rw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rw "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [Nat.add_succ, ih]All goals completed! The `[induction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#induction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic also supports user-defined induction principles with multiple targets (aka major premises). This example uses `[Nat.mod.inductionOn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___mod___inductionOn "Documentation for Nat.mod.inductionOn in Lean Language Reference")`, which has the following signature:
`[Nat.mod.inductionOn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___mod___inductionOn "Documentation for Nat.mod.inductionOn in Lean Language Reference")   {motive : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Sort u}   (x y  : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))   (ind  : ∀ x y, 0 < y ∧ y ≤ x → motive (x - y) y → motive x y)   (base : ∀ x y, ¬(0 < y ∧ y ≤ x) → motive x y) :   motive x y `

We close this section with one last tactic that is designed to facilitate working with inductive types, namely, the `[injection](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#injection "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic. By design, the elements of an inductive type are freely generated, which is to say, the constructors are injective and have disjoint ranges. The `[injection](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#injection "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic is designed to make use of this fact:

As the second example shows, the `[contradiction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#contradiction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic also detects contradictions of this form.
##  7.7. Inductive Families[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=inductive-families "Permalink")
We are almost done describing the full range of inductive definitions accepted by Lean. So far, you have seen that Lean allows you to introduce inductive types with any number of recursive constructors. In fact, a single inductive definition can introduce an indexed _family_ of inductive types, in a manner we now describe.
An inductive family is an indexed family of types defined by a simultaneous induction of the following form:

```
inductive foo : ... → Sort u where
  | constructor₁ : ... → foo ...
  | constructor₂ : ... → foo ...
  ...
  | constructorₙ : ... → foo ...

```

In contrast to an ordinary inductive definition, which constructs an element of some `Sort u`, the more general version constructs a function `... → ``Sort u`, where “`...`” denotes a sequence of argument types, also known as _indices_. Each constructor then constructs an element of some member of the family. One example is the definition of `Vect α n`, the type of vectors of elements of `α` of length `n`:
`inductive Vect (α : Type u) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Type u where   | nil  : Vect α 0   | cons : α → {n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")} → Vect α n → Vect α (n + 1) ```
Notice that the `cons` constructor takes an element of `Vect α n` and returns an element of `Vect α (n + 1)`, thereby using an element of one member of the family to build an element of another.
A more exotic example is given by the definition of the equality type in Lean:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`inductive Eq {α : Sort u} (a : α) : α → Prop where   | refl : [Eq](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Eq "Definition of example") a a `
`end Hidden `
For each fixed `α : Sort u` and `a : α`, this definition constructs a family of types `[Eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a x`, indexed by `x : α`. Notably, however, there is only one constructor, `refl`, which is an element of `[Eq](Inductive-Types/#Hidden___Eq "Definition of example") a a`. Intuitively, the only way to construct a proof of `[Eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a x` is to use reflexivity, in the case where `x` is `a`. Note that `[Eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a a` is the only inhabited type in the family of types `[Eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a x`. The elimination principle generated by Lean is as follows:
`set_option [pp.proofs](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Reading-Proof-States/#pp___proofs "Documentation for option pp.proofs in Lean Language Reference") true `
`[universe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Universes/#Lean___Parser___Command___universe-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") u v  ```@Eq.rec : {α : Sort u} →   {a : α} → {motive : (a_1 : α) → a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a_1 → Sort v} → motive a ([Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") a) → {a_1 : α} → (t : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a_1) → motive a_1 t`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (@Eq.rec : {α : Sort u} → {a : α} → {motive : (x : α) → a = x → Sort v} → motive a [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") → {b : α} → (h : a = b) → motive b h)`
```
@Eq.rec : {α : Sort u} →
  {a : α} → {motive : (a_1 : α) → a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a_1 → Sort v} → motive a ([Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") a) → {a_1 : α} → (t : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a_1) → motive a_1 t
```
``
It is a remarkable fact that all the basic axioms for equality follow from the constructor, `refl`, and the eliminator, `Eq.rec`. The definition of equality is atypical, however; see the discussion in [Axiomatic Details](Inductive-Types/#axiomatic-details).
The recursor `Eq.rec` is also used to define substitution:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`theorem subst {α : Type u} {a b : α} {p : α → Prop}     (h₁ : [Eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a b) (h₂ : p a) : p b :=   Eq.rec (motive := fun x _ => p x) h₂ h₁ `
`end Hidden `
You can also define `subst` using `match`.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `
`theorem subst {α : Type u} {a b : α} {p : α → Prop}     (h₁ : [Eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a b) (h₂ : p a) : p b :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") h₁ [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") => h₂ `
`end Hidden `
Actually, Lean compiles the `match` expressions using a definition based on generated helpers such as `Eq.casesOn` and `Eq.ndrec`, which are themselves defined using `Eq.rec`.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Hidden `

It is not hard to prove that `[Eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference")` is symmetric and transitive. In the type theory literature, there are further generalizations of inductive definitions, for example, the principles of _induction-recursion_ and _induction-induction_. These are not supported by Lean.
##  7.8. Axiomatic Details[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=axiomatic-details "Permalink")
We have described inductive types and their syntax through examples. This section provides additional information for those interested in the axiomatic foundations.
We have seen that the constructor to an inductive type takes _parameters_ —intuitively, the arguments that remain fixed throughout the inductive construction—and _indices_ , the arguments parameterizing the family of types that is simultaneously under construction. Each constructor should have a type, where the argument types are built up from previously defined types, the parameter and index types, and the inductive family currently being defined. The requirement is that if the latter is present at all, it occurs only _strictly positively_. This means simply that any argument to the constructor in which it occurs is a dependent arrow type in which the inductive type under definition occurs only as the resulting type, where the indices are given in terms of constants and previous arguments.
Since an inductive type lives in `Sort u` for some `u`, it is reasonable to ask _which_ universe levels `u` can be instantiated to. Each constructor `c` in the definition of a family `C` of inductive types is of the form

```
  c : (a : α) → (b : β[a]) → C a p[a,b]

```

where `a` is a sequence of data type parameters, `b` is the sequence of arguments to the constructors, and `p[a, b]` are the indices, which determine which element of the inductive family the construction inhabits. (Note that this description is somewhat misleading, in that the arguments to the constructor can appear in any order as long as the dependencies make sense.) The constraints on the universe level of `C` fall into two cases, depending on whether or not the inductive type is specified to land in `Prop` (that is, `Sort 0`).
Let us first consider the case where the inductive type is _not_ specified to land in `Prop`. Then the universe level `u` is constrained to satisfy the following:
> For each constructor `c` as above, and each `βk[a]` in the sequence `β[a]`, if `βk[a] : Sort v`, we have `u` ≥ `v`.
In other words, the universe level `u` is required to be at least as large as the universe level of each type that represents an argument to a constructor.
When the inductive type is specified to land in `Prop`, there are no constraints on the universe levels of the constructor arguments. But these universe levels do have a bearing on the elimination rule. Generally speaking, for an inductive type in `Prop`, the motive of the elimination rule is required to be in `Prop`.
There is an exception to this last rule: we are allowed to eliminate from an inductively defined `Prop` to an arbitrary `Sort` when there is only one constructor and each constructor argument is either in `Prop` or an index. The intuition is that in this case the elimination does not make use of any information that is not already given by the mere fact that the type of argument is inhabited. This special case is known as _singleton elimination_.
We have already seen singleton elimination at play in applications of `Eq.rec`, the eliminator for the inductively defined equality type. We can use an element `h : [Eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a b` to cast an element `h₂ : p a` to `p b` even when `p a` and `p b` are arbitrary types, because the cast does not produce new data; it only reinterprets the data we already have. Singleton elimination is also used with heterogeneous equality and well-founded recursion, which will be discussed in a the chapter on [induction and recursion](Induction-and-Recursion/#well-founded-recursion-and-induction).
##  7.9. Mutual and Nested Inductive Types[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=mutual-and-nested-inductive-types "Permalink")
We now consider two generalizations of inductive types that are often useful, which Lean supports by “compiling” them down to the more primitive kinds of inductive types described above. In other words, Lean parses the more general definitions, defines auxiliary inductive types based on them, and then uses the auxiliary types to define the ones we really want. Lean's equation compiler, described in the next chapter, is needed to make use of these types effectively. Nonetheless, it makes sense to describe the declarations here, because they are straightforward variations on ordinary inductive definitions.
First, Lean supports _mutually defined_ inductive types. The idea is that we can define two (or more) inductive types at the same time, where each one refers to the other(s).
`[mutual](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Definitions/Recursive-Definitions/#Lean___Parser___Command___mutual "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   inductive Even : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Prop where     | even_zero : [Even](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Introduction/#Even___zero-next "Documentation for Even in Lean Language Reference") 0     | even_succ : (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) → Odd n → [Even](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Introduction/#Even___zero-next "Documentation for Even in Lean Language Reference") (n + 1)    inductive Odd : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Prop where     | odd_succ : (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) → [Even](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Introduction/#Even___zero-next "Documentation for Even in Lean Language Reference") n → Odd (n + 1) [end](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Definitions/Recursive-Definitions/#Lean___Parser___Command___mutual "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ```
In this example, two types are defined simultaneously: a natural number `n` is `Even` if it is `0` or one more than an `Odd` number, and `Odd` if it is one more than an `Even` number. In the exercises below, you are asked to spell out the details.
A mutual inductive definition can also be used to define the notation of a finite tree with nodes labelled by elements of `α`:
`[mutual](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Definitions/Recursive-Definitions/#Lean___Parser___Command___mutual "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     inductive Tree (α : Type u) where       | node : α → [TreeList](Inductive-Types/#Tree "Definition of example") α → Tree α      inductive TreeList (α : Type u) where       | nil  : [TreeList](Inductive-Types/#Tree "Definition of example") α       | cons : Tree α → [TreeList](Inductive-Types/#Tree "Definition of example") α → [TreeList](Inductive-Types/#Tree "Definition of example") α [end](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Definitions/Recursive-Definitions/#Lean___Parser___Command___mutual "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ```
With this definition, one can construct an element of `Tree α` by giving an element of `α` together with a list of subtrees, possibly empty. The list of subtrees is represented by the type `[TreeList](Inductive-Types/#Tree "Definition of example") α`, which is defined to be either the empty list, `nil`, or the `cons` of a tree and an element of `[TreeList](Inductive-Types/#Tree "Definition of example") α`.
This definition is inconvenient to work with, however. It would be much nicer if the list of subtrees were given by the type `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") (Tree α)`, especially since Lean's library contains a number of functions and theorems for working with lists. One can show that the type `[TreeList](Inductive-Types/#Tree "Definition of example") α` is _isomorphic_ to `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") (Tree α)`, but translating results back and forth along this isomorphism is tedious.
In fact, Lean allows us to define the inductive type we really want:
`inductive Tree (α : Type u) where   | mk : α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") (Tree α) → Tree α ```
This is known as a _nested_ inductive type. It falls outside the strict specification of an inductive type given in the last section because `Tree` does not occur strictly positively among the arguments to `mk`, but, rather, nested inside the `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")` type constructor. Lean then automatically builds the isomorphism between `[TreeList](Inductive-Types/#Tree "Definition of example") α` and `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") (Tree α)` in its kernel, and defines the constructors for `Tree` in terms of the isomorphism.
##  7.10. Exercises
  1. Try defining other operations on the natural numbers, such as multiplication, the predecessor function (with `[pred](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___pred "Documentation for Nat.pred in Lean Language Reference") 0 = 0`), truncated subtraction (with `n - m = 0` when `m` is greater than or equal to `n`), and exponentiation. Then try proving some of their basic properties, building on the theorems we have already proved.
Since many of these are already defined in Lean's core library, you should work within a namespace named `Hidden`, or something like that, in order to avoid name clashes.
  2. Define some operations on lists, like a `length` function or the `reverse` function. Prove some properties, such as the following:
a. `length (xs ++ ys) = length xs + length ys`
b. `length (reverse xs) = length xs`
c. `reverse (reverse xs) = xs`
  3. Define an inductive data type consisting of terms built up from the following constructors:
     * `[const](Induction-and-Recursion/#Term-next "Definition of example") n`, a constant denoting the natural number `n`
     * `[var](Interacting-with-Lean/#Term "Definition of example") n`, a variable, numbered `n`
     * `plus s t`, denoting the sum of `s` and `t`
     * `times s t`, denoting the product of `s` and `t`
Recursively define a function that evaluates any such term with respect to an assignment of values to the variables.
  4. Similarly, define the type of propositional formulas, as well as functions on the type of such formulas: an evaluation function, functions that measure the complexity of a formula, and a function that substitutes another formula for a given variable.
