title: Quantifiers and Equality
markdown:
[←3. Propositions and Proofs](Propositions-and-Proofs/#propositions-and-proofs "3. Propositions and Proofs")[5. Tactics→](Tactics/#Theorem-Proving-in-Lean-4--Tactics "5. Tactics")
#  4. Quantifiers and Equality[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=quantifiers-and-equality "Permalink")
The last chapter introduced you to methods that construct proofs of statements involving the propositional connectives. In this chapter, we extend the repertoire of logical constructions to include the universal and existential quantifiers, and the equality relation.
##  4.1. The Universal Quantifier[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=the-universal-quantifier "Permalink")
Notice that if `α` is any type, we can represent a unary predicate `p` on `α` as an object of type `α → Prop`. In that case, given `x : α`, `p x` denotes the assertion that `p` holds of `x`. Similarly, an object `r : α → α → Prop` denotes a binary relation on `α`: given `x y : α`, `r x y` denotes the assertion that `x` is related to `y`.
The universal quantifier, `∀ x : α, p x` is supposed to denote the assertion that “for every `x : α`, `p x`” holds. As with the propositional connectives, in systems of natural deduction, “forall” is governed by an introduction and elimination rule. Informally, the introduction rule states:
> Given a proof of `p x`, in a context where `x : α` is arbitrary, we obtain a proof `∀ x : α, p x`.
The elimination rule states:
> Given a proof `∀ x : α, p x` and any term `t : α`, we obtain a proof of `p t`.
As was the case for implication, the propositions-as-types interpretation now comes into play. Remember the introduction and elimination rules for dependent arrow types:
> Given a term `t` of type `β x`, in a context where `x : α` is arbitrary, we have `(fun x : α => t) : (x : α) → β x`.
The elimination rule states:
> Given a term `s : (x : α) → β x` and any term `t : α`, we have `s t : β t`.
In the case where `p x` has type `Prop`, if we replace `(x : α) → β x` with `∀ x : α, p x`, we can read these as the correct rules for building proofs involving the universal quantifier.
The Calculus of Constructions therefore identifies dependent arrow types with forall-expressions in this way. If `p` is any expression, `∀ x : α, p` is nothing more than alternative notation for `(x : α) → p`, with the idea that the former is more natural than the latter in cases where `p` is a proposition. Typically, the expression `p` will depend on `x : α`. Recall that, in the case of ordinary function spaces, we could interpret `α → β` as the special case of `(x : α) → β` in which `β` does not depend on `x`. Similarly, we can think of an implication `p → q` between propositions as the special case of `∀ x : p, q` in which the expression `q` does not depend on `x`.
Here is an example of how the [propositions-as-types](Propositions-and-Proofs/#--tech-term-propositions-as-types) correspondence gets put into practice.
`example (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop) :     (∀ x : α, p x ∧ q x) → ∀ y : α, p y :=   fun h : ∀ x : α, p x ∧ q x =>   fun y : α =>   show p y from (h y).left ```
As a notational convention, we give the universal quantifier the widest scope possible, so parentheses are needed to limit the quantifier over `x` to the hypothesis in the example above. The canonical way to prove `∀ y : α, p y` is to take an arbitrary `y`, and prove `p y`. This is the introduction rule. Now, given that `h` has type `∀ x : α, p x ∧ q x`, the expression `h y` has type `p`` ``y`` ∧ ``q`` ``y`. This is the elimination rule. Taking the left conjunct gives the desired conclusion, `p y`.
Remember that expressions which differ up to renaming of bound variables are considered to be equivalent. So, for example, we could have used the same variable, `x`, in both the hypothesis and conclusion, and instantiated it by a different variable, `z`, in the proof:
`example (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop) :     (∀ x : α, p x ∧ q x) → ∀ x : α, p x :=   fun h : ∀ x : α, p x ∧ q x =>   fun z : α =>   show p z from [And.left](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.left in Lean Language Reference") (h z) ```
As another example, here is how we can express the fact that a relation, `r`, is transitive:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (r : α → α → Prop) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (trans_r : ∀ x y z, r x y → r y z → r x z)  ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b c : α) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (hab : r a b) (hbc : r b c)  ```trans_r : ∀ (x y z : α), r x y → r y z → r x z`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") trans_r`
```
trans_r : ∀ (x y z : α), r x y → r y z → r x z
```
` ```trans_r a b c : r a b → r b c → r a c`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") trans_r a b c`
```
trans_r a b c : r a b → r b c → r a c
```
` ```trans_r a b c hab : r b c → r a c`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") trans_r a b c hab`
```
trans_r a b c hab : r b c → r a c
```
` ```trans_r a b c hab hbc : r a c`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") trans_r a b c hab hbc`
```
trans_r a b c hab hbc : r a c
```
``
Think about what is going on here. When we instantiate `trans_r` at the values `a b c`, we end up with a proof of `r`` ``a b`` → ``r`` ``b c`` → ``r`` ``a`` ``c`. Applying this to the “hypothesis” `hab : r a b`, we get a proof of the implication `r`` ``b c`` → ``r`` ``a`` ``c`. Finally, applying it to the hypothesis `hbc` yields a proof of the conclusion `r`` ``a`` ``c`.
In situations like this, it can be tedious to supply the arguments `a b c`, when they can be inferred from `hab hbc`. For that reason, it is common to make these arguments implicit:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (r : α → α → Prop) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (trans_r : ∀ {x y z}, r x y → r y z → r x z)  ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b c : α) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (hab : r a b) (hbc : r b c)  ```trans_r : r ?m.4 ?m.5 → r ?m.5 ?m.6 → r ?m.4 ?m.6`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") trans_r`
```
trans_r : r ?m.4 ?m.5 → r ?m.5 ?m.6 → r ?m.4 ?m.6
```
` ```trans_r hab : r b ?m.6 → r a ?m.6`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") trans_r hab`
```
trans_r hab : r b ?m.6 → r a ?m.6
```
` ```trans_r hab hbc : r a c`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") trans_r hab hbc`
```
trans_r hab hbc : r a c
```
``
The advantage is that we can simply write `trans_r hab hbc` as a proof of `r`` ``a`` ``c`. A disadvantage is that Lean does not have enough information to infer the types of the arguments in the expressions `trans_r` and `trans_r hab`. The output of the first `#check` command is `r ?m.1 ?m.2 → r ?m.2 ?m.3 → r ?m.1 ?m.3`, indicating that the implicit arguments are unspecified in this case.
Here is an example of how we can carry out elementary reasoning with an equivalence relation:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (r : α → α → Prop)  ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (refl_r : ∀ x, r x x) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (symm_r : ∀ {x y}, r x y → r y x) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (trans_r : ∀ {x y z}, r x y → r y z → r x z)  ``example (a b c d : α) (hab : r a b) (hcb : r c b) (hcd : r c d) : r a d :=   trans_r (trans_r hab (symm_r hcb)) hcd ```
To get used to using universal quantifiers, you should try some of the exercises at the end of this section.
It is the typing rule for dependent arrow types, and the universal quantifier in particular, that distinguishes `Prop` from other types. Suppose we have `α : Sort i` and `β : Sort j`, where the expression `β` may depend on a variable `x : α`. Then `(x : α) → β` is an element of `Sort (imax i j)`, where `imax i j` is the maximum of `i` and `j` if `j` is not `0`, and `0` otherwise.
The idea is as follows. If `j` is not `0`, then `(x : α) → β` is an element of `Sort (max i j)`. In other words, the type of dependent functions from `α` to `β` “lives” in the universe whose index is the maximum of `i` and `j`. Suppose, however, that `β` is of `Sort 0`, that is, an element of `Prop`. In that case, `(x : α) → β` is an element of `Sort 0` as well, no matter which type universe `α` lives in. In other words, if `β` is a proposition depending on `α`, then `∀ x : α, β` is again a proposition. This reflects the interpretation of `Prop` as the type of propositions rather than data, and it is what makes `Prop` _impredicative_.
The term “predicative” stems from foundational developments around the turn of the twentieth century, when logicians such as Poincaré and Russell blamed set-theoretic paradoxes on the “vicious circles” that arise when we define a property by quantifying over a collection that includes the very property being defined. Notice that if `α` is any type, we can form the type `α → Prop` of all predicates on `α` (the “power type of `α`”). The impredicativity of `Prop` means that we can form propositions that quantify over `α → Prop`. In particular, we can define predicates on `α` by quantifying over all predicates on `α`, which is exactly the type of circularity that was once considered problematic.
##  4.2. Equality[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=equality "Permalink")
Let us now turn to one of the most fundamental relations defined in Lean's library, namely, the equality relation. In the chapter on [inductive types](Inductive-Types/#inductive-types), we will explain _how_ equality is defined from the primitives of Lean's logical framework. In the meanwhile, here we explain how to use it.
Of course, a fundamental property of equality is that it is an equivalence relation:
``[Eq.refl.{u_1}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") {α : Sort u_1} (a : α) : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference")`
```
[Eq.refl.{u_1}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") {α : Sort u_1} (a : α) : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a
```
` ```[Eq.symm.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference") {α : Sort u} {a b : α} (h : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") b) : b [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Eq.symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference")`
```
[Eq.symm.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference") {α : Sort u} {a b : α} (h : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") b) : b [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a
```
` ```[Eq.trans.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") {α : Sort u} {a b c : α} (h₁ : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") b) (h₂ : b [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") c) : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") c`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Eq.trans`
```
[Eq.trans.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") {α : Sort u} {a b c : α} (h₁ : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") b) (h₂ : b [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") c) : a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") c
```
``
We can make the output easier to read by telling Lean not to insert the implicit arguments (which are displayed here as metavariables).
`[universe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Universes/#Lean___Parser___Command___universe-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") u  ```[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference")[Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") : ∀ {α : Sort u} (a : α), a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @[Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference").{u}`
```
[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference")[Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") : ∀ {α : Sort u} (a : α), a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a

` ```[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference")[Eq.symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference") : ∀ {α : Sort u} {a b : α}, a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") b → b [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @[Eq.symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference").{u}`
```
[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference")[Eq.symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference") : ∀ {α : Sort u} {a b : α}, a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") b → b [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a
```
` ```[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference")[Eq.trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") : ∀ {α : Sort u} {a b c : α}, a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") b → b [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") c → a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") c`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @[Eq.trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference").{u}`
```
[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference")[Eq.trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") : ∀ {α : Sort u} {a b c : α}, a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") b → b [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") c → a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") c
```
``
The inscription `.{u}` tells Lean to instantiate the constants at the universe `u`.
Thus, for example, we can specialize the example from the previous section to the equality relation:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (a b c d : α) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (hab : a = b) (hcb : c = b) (hcd : c = d)  ``example : a = d :=   [Eq.trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") ([Eq.trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") hab ([Eq.symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference") hcb)) hcd ```
We can also use the projection notation:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (a b c d : α) [variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (hab : a = b) (hcb : c = b) (hcd : c = d) `
`example : a = d := (hab.[trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") hcb.[symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference")).[trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") hcd ```
Reflexivity is more powerful than it looks. Recall that terms in the Calculus of Constructions have a computational interpretation, and that the logical framework treats terms with a common reduct as the same. As a result, some nontrivial identities can be proved by reflexivity:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α β : Type)  ``example (f : α → β) (a : α) : (fun x => f x) a = f a := [Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") _ ``example (a : α) (b : β) : (a, b).1 = a := [Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") _ ``example : 2 + 3 = 5 := [Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") _ ```
This feature of the framework is so important that the library defines a notation `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")` for `[Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") _`:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α β : Type) `
`example (f : α → β) (a : α) : (fun x => f x) a = f a := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") ``example (a : α) (b : β) : (a, b).1 = a := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") ``example : 2 + 3 = 5 := rfl ```
Equality is much more than an equivalence relation, however. It has the important property that every assertion respects the equivalence, in the sense that we can substitute equal expressions without changing the truth value. That is, given `h1 : a = b` and `h2 : p a`, we can construct a proof for `p b` using substitution: `[Eq.subst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___subst "Documentation for Eq.subst in Lean Language Reference") h1 h2`.
`example (α : Type) (a b : α) (p : α → Prop)         (h1 : a = b) (h2 : p a) : p b :=   [Eq.subst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___subst "Documentation for Eq.subst in Lean Language Reference") h1 h2  ``example (α : Type) (a b : α) (p : α → Prop)     (h1 : a = b) (h2 : p a) : p b :=   h1 ▸ h2 ```
The triangle in the second presentation is a macro built on top of `[Eq.subst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___subst "Documentation for Eq.subst in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Eq.symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference")`, and you can enter it by typing `\t`.
The rule `[Eq.subst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___subst "Documentation for Eq.subst in Lean Language Reference")` is used to define the following auxiliary rules, which carry out more explicit substitutions. They are designed to deal with applicative terms, that is, terms of form `s t`. Specifically, `[congrArg](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#congrArg "Documentation for congrArg in Lean Language Reference")` can be used to replace the argument, `[congrFun](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#congrFun "Documentation for congrFun in Lean Language Reference")` can be used to replace the term that is being applied, and `[congr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#congr-next "Documentation for congr in Lean Language Reference")` can be used to replace both at once.
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b : α) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (f g : α → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (h₁ : a = b) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (h₂ : f = g)  ``example : f a = f b := [congrArg](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#congrArg "Documentation for congrArg in Lean Language Reference") f h₁ ``example : f a = g a := [congrFun](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#congrFun "Documentation for congrFun in Lean Language Reference") h₂ a ``example : f a = g b := [congr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#congr-next "Documentation for congr in Lean Language Reference") h₂ h₁ ```
Lean's library contains a large number of common identities, such as these:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b c : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))  ``example : a + 0 = a := Nat.add_zero a ``example : 0 + a = a := Nat.zero_add a ``example : a * 1 = a := Nat.mul_one a ``example : 1 * a = a := Nat.one_mul a ``example : a + b = b + a := Nat.add_comm a b ``example : a + b + c = a + (b + c) := Nat.add_assoc a b c ``example : a * b = b * a := Nat.mul_comm a b ``example : a * b * c = a * (b * c) := Nat.mul_assoc a b c ``example : a * (b + c) = a * b + a * c := Nat.mul_add a b c ``example : a * (b + c) = a * b + a * c := Nat.left_distrib a b c ``example : (a + b) * c = a * c + b * c := Nat.add_mul a b c ``example : (a + b) * c = a * c + b * c := Nat.right_distrib a b c ```
Note that `Nat.mul_add` and `Nat.add_mul` are alternative names for `Nat.left_distrib` and `Nat.right_distrib`, respectively. The properties above are stated for the natural numbers (type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`).
Here is an example of a calculation in the natural numbers that uses substitution combined with associativity and distributivity.
`example (x y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) :     (x + y) * (x + y) =     x * x + y * x + x * y + y * y :=   have h1 : (x + y) * (x + y) = (x + y) * x + (x + y) * y :=     Nat.mul_add (x + y) x y   have h2 : (x + y) * (x + y) = x * x + y * x + (x * y + y * y) :=     (Nat.add_mul x y x) ▸ (Nat.add_mul x y y) ▸ h1   h2.[trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___trans "Documentation for Eq.trans in Lean Language Reference") (Nat.add_assoc (x * x + y * x) (x * y) (y * y)).[symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference") ```
Notice that the second implicit parameter to `[Eq.subst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___subst "Documentation for Eq.subst in Lean Language Reference")`, which provides the context in which the substitution is to occur, has type `α → Prop`. Inferring this predicate therefore requires an instance of _higher-order unification_. In full generality, the problem of determining whether a higher-order unifier exists is undecidable, and Lean can at best provide imperfect and approximate solutions to the problem. As a result, `[Eq.subst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___subst "Documentation for Eq.subst in Lean Language Reference")` doesn't always do what you want it to. The macro `h ▸ e` uses more effective heuristics for computing this implicit parameter, and often succeeds in situations where applying `[Eq.subst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___subst "Documentation for Eq.subst in Lean Language Reference")` fails.
Because equational reasoning is so common and important, Lean provides a number of mechanisms to carry it out more effectively. The next section offers syntax that allow you to write calculational proofs in a more natural and perspicuous way. But, more importantly, equational reasoning is supported by a term rewriter, a simplifier, and other kinds of automation. The term rewriter and simplifier are described briefly in the next section, and then in greater detail in the next chapter.
##  4.3. Calculational Proofs[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=calculational-proofs "Permalink")
A calculational proof is just a chain of intermediate results that are meant to be composed by basic principles such as the transitivity of equality. In Lean, a calculational proof starts with the keyword `calc`, and has the following syntax:

```
calc
  <expr>_0  'op_1'  <expr>_1  ':='  <proof>_1
  '_'       'op_2'  <expr>_2  ':='  <proof>_2
  ...
  '_'       'op_n'  <expr>_n  ':='  <proof>_n

```

Note that the `calc` relations all have the same indentation. Each `<proof>_i` is a proof for `<expr>_{i-1} op_i <expr>_i`.
We can also use `_` in the first relation (right after `<expr>_0`) which is useful to align the sequence of relation/proof pairs:

```
calc <expr>_0
    '_' 'op_1' <expr>_1 ':=' <proof>_1
    '_' 'op_2' <expr>_2 ':=' <proof>_2
    ...
    '_' 'op_n' <expr>_n ':=' <proof>_n

```

Here is an example:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b c d e : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))  ``theorem T     (h1 : a = b)     (h2 : b = c + 1)     (h3 : c = d)     (h4 : e = 1 + d) :     a = e :=   calc     a = b      := h1     _ = c + 1  := h2     _ = d + 1  := [congrArg](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#congrArg "Documentation for congrArg in Lean Language Reference") [Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") h3     _ = 1 + d  := Nat.add_comm d 1     _ = e      := [Eq.symm](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___symm "Documentation for Eq.symm in Lean Language Reference") h4 ```
This style of writing proofs is most effective when it is used in conjunction with the `simp` and `rw` tactics, which are discussed in greater detail in the next chapter. For example, using `rw` for rewrite, the proof above could be written as follows:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b c d e : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) `
`theorem T     (h1 : a = b)     (h2 : b = c + 1)     (h3 : c = d)     (h4 : e = 1 + d) :     a = e :=   calc     a = b      := Essentially, the `rw` tactic uses a given equality (which can be a hypothesis, a theorem name, or a complex term) to “rewrite” the goal. If doing so reduces the goal to an identity `t = t`, the tactic applies reflexivity to prove it.
Rewrites can be applied sequentially, so that the proof above can be shortened to this:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b c d e : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) `
`theorem T     (h1 : a = b)     (h2 : b = c + 1)     (h3 : c = d)     (h4 : e = 1 + d) :     a = e :=   calc     a = d + 1  := The `simp` tactic, instead, rewrites the goal by applying the given identities repeatedly, in any order, anywhere they are applicable in a term. It also uses other rules that have been previously declared to the system, and applies commutativity wisely to avoid looping. As a result, we can also prove the theorem as follows:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b c d e : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) `
`theorem T     (h1 : a = b)     (h2 : b = c + 1)     (h3 : c = d)     (h4 : e = 1 + d) :     a = e :=   bya:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero We will discuss variations of `rw` and `simp` in the next chapter.
The `calc` command can be configured for any relation that supports some form of transitivity. It can even combine different relations.
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (a b c d : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) ``example (h1 : a = b) (h2 : b ≤ c) (h3 : c + 1 < d) : a < d :=   calc     a = b     := h1     _ < b + 1 := Nat.lt_succ_self b     _ ≤ c + 1 := Nat.succ_le_succ h2     _ < d     := h3 ```
You can “teach” `calc` new transitivity theorems by adding new instances of the `[Trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#Trans___mk "Documentation for Trans in Lean Language Reference")` type class. Type classes are introduced later, but the following small example demonstrates how to extend the `calc` notation using new `[Trans](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#Trans___mk "Documentation for Trans in Lean Language Reference")` instances.

example") .. ```
The example above also makes it clear that you can use `calc` even if you do not have an infix notation for your relation. Lean already includes the standard Unicode notation for divisibility (using `∣`, which can be entered as `\dvd` or `\mid`), so the example above uses the ordinary vertical bar to avoid a conflict. In practice, this is not a good idea, as it risks confusion with the ASCII `|` used in the `match`` ... ``with` expression.

##  4.4. The Existential Quantifier[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=the-existential-quantifier "Permalink")
Finally, consider the existential quantifier, which can be written as either `exists x : α, p x` or `∃ x : α, p x`. Both versions are actually notationally convenient abbreviations for a more long-winded expression, `[Exists](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists in Lean Language Reference") (fun x : α => p x)`, defined in Lean's library.
As you should by now expect, the library includes both an introduction rule and an elimination rule. The introduction rule is straightforward: to prove `∃ x : α, p x`, it suffices to provide a suitable term `t` and a proof of `p t`. Here are some examples:
`example : ∃ x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), x > 0 :=   have h : 1 > 0 := Nat.zero_lt_succ 0   [Exists.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language Reference") 1 h  ``example (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (h : x > 0) : ∃ y, y < x :=   [Exists.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language Reference") 0 h  ``example (x y z : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (hxy : x < y) (hyz : y < z) : ∃ w, x < w ∧ w < z :=   [Exists.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language Reference") y ([And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hxy hyz)  ```[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language α → Prop} (w : α), p w → [Exists](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists in Lean Language Reference") We can use the anonymous constructor notation `⟨t, h⟩` for `[Exists.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language Reference") t h`, when the type is clear from the context.
`example : ∃ x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), x > 0 :=   have h : 1 > 0 := Nat.zero_lt_succ 0   ⟨1, h⟩  ``example (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (h : x > 0) : ∃ y, y < x :=   ⟨0, h⟩  ``example (x y z : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (hxy : x < y) (hyz : y < z) : ∃ w, x < w ∧ w < z :=   ⟨y, hxy, hyz⟩ ```
Note that `[Exists.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language Reference")` has implicit arguments: Lean has to infer the predicate `p : α → Prop` in the conclusion `∃ x, p x`. This is not a trivial affair. For example, if we have `hg : g 0 0 = 0` and write `[Exists.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language Reference") 0 hg`, there are many possible values for the predicate `p`, corresponding to the theorems `∃ x, g x x = x`, `∃ x, g x x = 0`, `∃ x, g x 0 = x`, etc. Lean uses the context to infer which one is appropriate. This is illustrated in the following example, in which we set the option `pp.explicit` to true to ask Lean's pretty-printer to show the implicit arguments.
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (g : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))  ``theorem gex1 (hg : g 0 0 = 0) : ∃ x, g x x = x := ⟨0, hg⟩ ``theorem gex2 (hg : g 0 0 = 0) : ∃ x, g x 0 = x := ⟨0, hg⟩ ``theorem gex3 (hg : g 0 0 = 0) : ∃ x, g 0 0 = x := ⟨0, hg⟩ ``theorem gex4 (hg : g 0 0 = 0) : ∃ x, g x x = 0 := ⟨0, hg⟩  ``set_option ``
We can view `[Exists.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language Reference")` as an information-hiding operation, since it hides the witness to the body of the assertion. The existential elimination rule, `Exists.elim`, performs the opposite operation. It allows us to prove a proposition `q` from `∃ x : α, p x`, by showing that `q` follows from `p w` for an arbitrary value `w`. Roughly speaking, since we know there is an `x` satisfying `p x`, we can give it a name, say, `w`. If `q` does not mention `w`, then showing that `q` follows from `p w` is tantamount to showing that `q` follows from the existence of any such `x`. Here is an example:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop)  ``example (h : ∃ x, p x ∧ q x) : ∃ x, q x ∧ p x :=   Exists.elim h     (fun w =>      fun hw : p w ∧ q w =>      show ∃ x, q x ∧ p x from ⟨w, hw.[right](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.right in Lean Language Reference"), hw.[left](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.left in Lean Language Reference")⟩) ```
It may be helpful to compare the exists-elimination rule to the or-elimination rule: the assertion `∃ x : α, p x` can be thought of as a big disjunction of the propositions `p a`, as `a` ranges over all the elements of `α`. Note that the anonymous constructor notation `⟨w, hw.[right](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.right in Lean Language Reference"), hw.[left](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.left in Lean Language Reference")⟩` abbreviates a nested constructor application; we could equally well have written `⟨``w``, ⟨``hw.[right](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.right in Lean Language Reference")``, ``hw.[left](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.left in Lean Language Reference")``⟩⟩`.
Notice that an existential proposition is very similar to a sigma type, as described in dependent types section. The difference is that existential propositions are _propositions_ , while sigma types are _types_. Otherwise, they are very similar. Given a predicate `p : α → Prop` and a family of types `β : α → Type`, for a term `a : α` with `h : p a` and `h' : β a`, the term `[Exists.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Quantifiers/#Exists___intro "Documentation for Exists.intro in Lean Language Reference") a h` has type `(∃ x : α, p x) : Prop`, while `[Sigma.mk](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Sigma___mk "Documentation for Sigma.mk in Lean Language Reference") a h'` has type `(Σ x : α, β x)`. The similarity between `∃` and `Σ` is another instance of the [Curry-Howard isomorphism](Propositions-and-Proofs/#--tech-term-Curry-Howard-isomorphism).
Lean provides a more convenient way to eliminate from an existential quantifier with the `match` expression:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop)  ``example (h : ∃ x, p x ∧ q x) : ∃ x, q x ∧ p x :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") h [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | ⟨w, hw⟩ => ⟨w, hw.[right](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.right in Lean Language Reference"), hw.[left](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.left in Lean Language Reference")⟩ ```
The `match` expression is part of Lean's function definition system, which provides convenient and expressive ways of defining complex functions. Once again, it is the [Curry-Howard isomorphism](Propositions-and-Proofs/#--tech-term-Curry-Howard-isomorphism) that allows us to co-opt this mechanism for writing proofs as well. The `match` statement “destructs” the existential assertion into the components `w` and `hw`, which can then be used in the body of the statement to prove the proposition. We can annotate the types used in the match for greater clarity:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop) `
`example (h : ∃ x, p x ∧ q x) : ∃ x, q x ∧ p x :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") h [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | ⟨(w : α), (hw : p w ∧ q w)⟩ => ⟨w, hw.[right](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.right in Lean Language Reference"), hw.[left](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.left in Lean Language Reference")⟩ ```
We can even use the match statement to decompose the conjunction at the same time:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop) `
`example (h : ∃ x, p x ∧ q x) : ∃ x, q x ∧ p x :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") h [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | ⟨w, hpw, hqw⟩ => ⟨w, hqw, hpw⟩ ```
Lean also provides a pattern-matching `let` expression:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop) `
`example (h : ∃ x, p x ∧ q x) : ∃ x, q x ∧ p x :=   let ⟨w, hpw, hqw⟩ := h   ⟨w, hqw, hpw⟩ ```
This is essentially just alternative notation for the `match` construct above. Lean will even allow us to use an implicit `match` in the `fun` expression:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop) `
`example : (∃ x, p x ∧ q x) → ∃ x, q x ∧ p x :=   fun ⟨w, hpw, hqw⟩ => ⟨w, hqw, hpw⟩ ```
We will see in [Induction and Recursion](Induction-and-Recursion/#induction-and-recursion) that all these variations are instances of a more general pattern-matching construct.
In the following example, we define `[IsEven](Quantifiers-and-Equality/#IsEven "Definition of example") a` as `∃ b, a = 2 * b`, and then we show that the sum of two even numbers is an even number.
`def IsEven (a : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) := ∃ b, a = 2 * b  ``theorem even_plus_even (h1 : [IsEven](Quantifiers-and-Equality/#IsEven "Definition of example") a) (h2 : [IsEven](Quantifiers-and-Equality/#IsEven "Definition of example") b) : Just as the constructive “or” is stronger than the classical “or,” so, too, is the constructive “exists” stronger than the classical “exists”. For example, the following implication requires classical reasoning because, from a constructive standpoint, knowing that it is not the case that every `x` satisfies `¬ p` is not the same as having a particular `x` that satisfies `p`.
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Classical ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (p : α → Prop)  ``example (h : ¬ ∀ x, ¬ p x) : ∃ x, p x :=   byContradiction     (fun h1 : ¬ ∃ x, p x =>       have h2 : ∀ x, ¬ p x :=         fun x =>         fun h3 : p x =>         have h4 : ∃ x, p x := ⟨x, h3⟩         show [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference") from h1 h4       show [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference") from h h2) ```
What follows are some common identities involving the existential quantifier. In the exercises below, we encourage you to prove as many as you can. We also leave it to you to determine which are nonconstructive, and hence require some form of classical reasoning.
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Classical  ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (r : Prop)  ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∃ x : α, r) → r := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example (a : α) : r → (∃ x : α, r) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∃ x, p x ∧ r) ↔ (∃ x, p x) ∧ r := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∃ x, p x ∨ q x) ↔ (∃ x, p x) ∨ (∃ x, q x) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∀ x, p x) ↔ ¬ (∃ x, ¬ p x) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∃ x, p x) ↔ ¬ (∀ x, ¬ p x) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (¬ ∃ x, p x) ↔ (∀ x, ¬ p x) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (¬ ∀ x, p x) ↔ (∃ x, ¬ p x) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∀ x, p x → r) ↔ (∃ x, p x) → r := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example (a : α) : (∃ x, p x → r) ↔ (∀ x, p x) → r := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example (a : α) : (∃ x, r → p x) ↔ (r → ∃ x, p x) := sorry ```
Notice that the second example and the last two examples require the assumption that there is at least one element `a` of type `α`.

##  4.5. More on the Proof Language[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=more-on-the-proof-language "Permalink")
We have seen that keywords like `fun`, `have`, and `show` make it possible to write formal proof terms that mirror the structure of informal mathematical proofs. In this section, we discuss some additional features of the proof language that are often convenient.
To start with, we can use anonymous `have` expressions to introduce an auxiliary goal without having to label it. We can refer to the last expression introduced in this way using the keyword `this`:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (f : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (h : ∀ x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), f x ≤ f (x + 1))  ``example : f 0 ≤ f 3 :=   have : f 0 ≤ f 1 := h 0   have : f 0 ≤ f 2 := Nat.le_trans this (h 1)   show f 0 ≤ f 3 from Nat.le_trans this (h 2) ```
Often proofs move from one fact to the next, so this can be effective in eliminating the clutter of lots of labels.
When the goal can be inferred, we can also ask Lean instead to fill in the proof by writing `by assumption`:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (f : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) [variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (h : ∀ x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), f x ≤ f (x + 1)) `
`example : f 0 ≤ f 3 :=   have : f 0 ≤ f 1 := h 0   have : f 0 ≤ f 2 := Nat.le_trans (byf:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h:∀ (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")), f x [≤](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") f [(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference")x [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference")this:f 0 [≤](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") f 1 := h 0⊢ f 0 [≤](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") f 1 [assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙) (h 1)   show f 0 ≤ f 3 from Nat.le_trans (byf:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h:∀ (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")), f x [≤](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") f [(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference")x [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference")this✝:f 0 [≤](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") f 1 := h 0this:f 0 [≤](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") f 2 := Nat.le_trans this✝ (h 1)⊢ f 0 [≤](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") f 2 [assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙) (h 2) ```
This tells Lean to use the `[assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic, which, in turn, proves the goal by finding a suitable hypothesis in the local context. We will learn more about the `[assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic in the next chapter.
We can also ask Lean to fill in the proof by writing `‹p›`, where `p` is the proposition whose proof we want Lean to find in the context. You can type these corner quotes using `\f<` and `\f>`, respectively. The letter “f” is for “French,” since the Unicode symbols can also be used as French quotation marks. In fact, the notation is defined in Lean as follows:
`[notation](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Notations-and-Macros/Notations/#Lean___Parser___Command___notation "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "‹" p "›" => show p by [assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") ```
This approach is more robust than using `by [assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, because the type of the assumption that needs to be inferred is given explicitly. It also makes proofs more readable. Here is a more elaborate example:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (f : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (h : ∀ x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), f x ≤ f (x + 1))  ``example : f 0 ≥ f 1 → f 1 ≥ f 2 → f 0 = f 2 :=   fun _ : f 0 ≥ f 1 =>   fun _ : f 1 ≥ f 2 =>   have : f 0 ≥ f 2 := Nat.le_trans ‹f 1 ≥ f 2› ‹f 0 ≥ f 1›   have : f 0 ≤ f 2 := Nat.le_trans (h 0) (h 1)   show f 0 = f 2 from Nat.le_antisymm this ‹f 0 ≥ f 2› ```
Keep in mind that you can use the French quotation marks in this way to refer to _anything_ in the context, not just things that were introduced anonymously. Its use is also not limited to propositions, though using it for data is somewhat odd:
`example (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := ‹Nat› ```
Later, we show how you can extend the proof language using the Lean macro system.
##  4.6. Exercises
  1. Prove these equivalences:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop)  ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∀ x, p x ∧ q x) ↔ (∀ x, p x) ∧ (∀ x, q x) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∀ x, p x → q x) → (∀ x, p x) → (∀ x, q x) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∀ x, p x) ∨ (∀ x, q x) → ∀ x, p x ∨ q x := sorry ```
You should also try to understand why the reverse implication is not derivable in the last example.
  2. It is often possible to bring a component of a formula outside a universal quantifier, when it does not depend on the quantified variable. Try proving these (one direction of the second of these requires classical logic):
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (α : Type) (p q : α → Prop) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (r : Prop)  ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : α → ((∀ x : α, r) ↔ r) := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∀ x, p x ∨ r) ↔ (∀ x, p x) ∨ r := sorry ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example : (∀ x, r → p x) ↔ (r → ∀ x, p x) := sorry ```
  3. Consider the “barber paradox,” that is, the claim that in a certain town there is a (male) barber that shaves all and only the men who do not shave themselves. Prove that this is a contradiction:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (men : Type) (barber : men) ``[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (shaves : men → men → Prop)  ```declaration uses 'sorry'`example (h : ∀ x : men, shaves barber x ↔ ¬ shaves x x) : [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference") := sorry ```
  4. Remember that, without any parameters, an expression of type `Prop` is just an assertion. Fill in the definitions of `prime` and `Fermat_prime` below, and construct each of the given assertions. For example, you can say that there are infinitely many primes by asserting that for every natural number `n`, there is a prime number greater than `n`. Goldbach's weak conjecture states that every odd number greater than 5 is the sum of three primes. Look up the definition of a Fermat prime or any of the other statements, if necessary.
`def `declaration uses 'sorry'`even (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : Prop := sorry ``def `declaration uses 'sorry'`prime (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : Prop := sorry ``def `declaration uses 'sorry'`infinitely_many_primes : Prop := sorry ``def `declaration uses 'sorry'`Fermat_prime (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : Prop := sorry ``def `declaration uses 'sorry'`infinitely_many_Fermat_primes : Prop := sorry ``def `declaration uses 'sorry'`goldbach_conjecture : Prop := sorry ``def `declaration uses 'sorry'`Goldbach's_weak_conjecture : Prop := sorry ``def `declaration uses 'sorry'`Fermat's_last_theorem : Prop := sorry ```
  5. Prove as many of the identities listed in the Existential Quantifier section as you can.
