title: Structures and Records
markdown:
[←8. Induction and Recursion](Induction-and-Recursion/#induction-and-recursion "8. Induction and Recursion")[10. Type Classes→](Type-Classes/#type-classes "10. Type Classes")
#  9. Structures and Records[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=structures-and-records "Permalink")
We have seen that Lean's foundational system includes inductive types. We have, moreover, noted that it is a remarkable fact that it is possible to construct a substantial edifice of mathematics based on nothing more than the type universes, dependent arrow types, and inductive types; everything else follows from those. The Lean standard library contains many instances of inductive types (e.g., `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Prod](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod in Lean Language Reference")`, `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`), and even the logical connectives are defined using inductive types.
Recall that a non-recursive inductive type that contains only one constructor is called a _structure_ or _record_. The product type is a structure, as is the dependent product (Sigma) type. In general, whenever we define a structure `S`, we usually define _projection_ functions that allow us to “destruct” each instance of `S` and retrieve the values that are stored in its fields. The functions `[Prod.fst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.fst in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Prod.snd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.snd in Lean Language Reference")`, which return the first and second elements of a pair, are examples of such projections.
When writing programs or formalizing mathematics, it is not uncommon to define structures containing many fields. The `structure` command, available in Lean, provides infrastructure to support this process. When we define a structure using this command, Lean automatically generates all the projection functions. The `structure` command also allows us to define new structures based on previously defined ones. Moreover, Lean provides convenient notation for defining instances of a given structure.
##  9.1. Declaring Structures[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=declaring-structures "Permalink")
The structure command is essentially a “front end” for defining inductive data types. Every `structure` declaration introduces a namespace with the same name. The general form is as follows:

```
    structure <name> <parameters> <parent-structures> where
      <constructor> :: <fields>

```

Most parts are optional. Here is an example:
`structure Point (α : Type u) where   mk ::   x : α   y : α ```
Values of type `Point` are created using `[Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") a b`, and the fields of a point `p` are accessed using `Point.x p` and `Point.y p` (but `p.x` and `p.y` also work, see below). The structure command also generates useful recursors and theorems. Here are some of the constructions generated for the declaration above.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where   mk ::   x : α   y : α `
`-- a Type `Point.{u} (α : Type u) : Type u`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Point`
```
Point.{u} (α : Type u) : Type u
```
` ``-- the eliminator `@Point.rec : {α : Type u_2} →   {motive : Point α → Sort u_1} → ((x y : α) → motive [{](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x[,](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [}](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example")) → (t : Point α) → motive t`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @Point.rec`
```
@Point.rec : {α : Type u_2} →
  {motive : Point α → Sort u_1} → ((x y : α) → motive [{](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x[,](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [}](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example")) → (t : Point α) → motive t
```
` ``-- the constructor `[@](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example")[Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") : {α : Type u_1} → α → α → Point α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @[Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example")`
```
[@](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example")[Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") : {α : Type u_1} → α → α → Point α
```
` ``-- a projection `@Point.x : {α : Type u_1} → Point α → α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @Point.x`
```
@Point.x : {α : Type u_1} → Point α → α
```
` ``-- a projection `@Point.y : {α : Type u_1} → Point α → α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @Point.y`
```
@Point.y : {α : Type u_1} → Point α → α
```
``
If the constructor name is not provided, then a constructor is named `mk` by default.
Here are some simple theorems and expressions that use the generated constructions. As usual, you can avoid the prefix `Point` by using the command `[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Point`.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where   x : α   y : α `
``10`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Point.x ([Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10 20)`
```
10
```
` ```20`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Point.y ([Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10 20)`
```
20
```
` ``[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Point  ``example (a b : α) : x ([mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") a b) = a :=   [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")  ``example (a b : α) : y ([mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") a b) = b :=   rfl ```
Given `p : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, the dot notation `p.x` is shorthand for `Point.x p`. This provides a convenient way of accessing the fields of a structure.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where  x : α  y : α `
`def p := [Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10 20  ```p.x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p.x`
```
p.x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``10`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p.x`
```
10
```
``20`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p.y`
```
20
```
``
The dot notation is convenient not just for accessing the projections of a record, but also for applying functions defined in a namespace with the same name. Recall from the [Conjunction section](Propositions-and-Proofs/#conjunction) if `p` has type `Point`, the expression `p.foo` is interpreted as `Point.foo p`, assuming that the first non-implicit argument to `foo` has type `Point`. The expression `p.[add](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next "Definition of example") q` is therefore shorthand for `Point.add p q` in the example below.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where   x : α   y : α deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")  ``def Point.add (p q : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) :=   [mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") (p.x + q.x) (p.y + q.y)  ``def p : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := [Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 1 2 ``def q : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := [Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 3 4  ```{ x := 4, y := 6 }`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p.[add](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next "Definition of example") q`
```
{ x := 4, y := 6 }
```
``
In the next chapter, you will learn how to define a function like `[add](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next "Definition of example")` so that it works generically for elements of `Point α` rather than just `Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, assuming `α` has an associated addition operation.
More generally, given an expression `p.foo x y z` where `p : Point`, Lean will insert `p` at the first argument to `Point.foo` of type `Point`. For example, with the definition of scalar multiplication below, `p.[smul](Structures-and-Records/#Point___smul "Definition of example") 3` is interpreted as `[Point.smul](Structures-and-Records/#Point___smul "Definition of example") 3 p`.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where  x : α  y : α  deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference") `
`def Point.smul (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (p : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) :=   [Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") (n * p.x) (n * p.y)  ``def p : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := [Point.mk](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 1 2  ```{ x := 3, y := 6 }`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p.[smul](Structures-and-Records/#Point___smul "Definition of example") 3`
```
{ x := 3, y := 6 }
```
` ``example {p : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")} : p.[smul](Structures-and-Records/#Point___smul "Definition of example") 3 = [Point.smul](Structures-and-Records/#Point___smul "Definition of example") 3 p := rfl ```
It is common to use a similar trick with the `[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")` function, which takes a list as its second non-implicit argument:
``[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") : {α : Type u_1} → {β : Type u_2} → (α → β) → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") β`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")`
```
[@](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") : {α : Type u_1} → {β : Type u_2} → (α → β) → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") β
```
` ``def xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := [1, 2, 3] ``def f : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := fun x => x * x  ```[1, 4, 9]`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [xs](Structures-and-Records/#xs "Definition of example").[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") f`
```
[1, 4, 9]
```
` ``example {xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α} {f : α → β} :     xs.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") f = [List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") f xs :=   rfl ```
Here `[xs](Structures-and-Records/#xs "Definition of example").[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") f` is interpreted as `[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") f xs`.
##  9.2. Objects[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=objects "Permalink")
We have been using constructors to create elements of a structure type. For structures containing many fields, this is often inconvenient, because we have to remember the order in which the fields were defined. Lean therefore provides the following alternative notations for defining elements of a structure type.

```
    { (<field-name> := <expr>)* : structure-type }
    or
    { (<field-name> := <expr>)* }

```

The suffix `: structure-type` can be omitted whenever the name of the structure can be inferred from the expected type. For example, we use this notation to define “points.” The order that the fields are specified does not matter, so all the expressions below define the same point.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where   x : α   y : α  ```[{](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10[,](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 20 [}](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") { x := 10, y := 20 : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") }`
```
[{](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10[,](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 20 [}](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
` ```[{](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10[,](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 20 [}](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") { y := 20, x := 10 : Point _ }`
```
[{](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10[,](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 20 [}](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
` ```[{](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10[,](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 20 [}](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ({ x := 10, y := 20 } : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
[{](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") x [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 10[,](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") y [:=](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") 20 [}](Structures-and-Records/#Point "Definition of example") : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
` ``example : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   { y := 20, x := 10 } ```
Fields can be marked as implicit using curly braces. Implicit fields become implicit parameters to the constructor.
If the value of a field is not specified, Lean tries to infer it. If the unspecified fields cannot be inferred, Lean flags an error indicating the corresponding placeholder could not be synthesized.
`structure MyStruct where     {α : Type u}     {β : Type v}     a : α     b : β  ```{ [α](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), [β](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference"), [a](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := 10, [b](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference") } : [MyStruct](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") { [a](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := 10, [b](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference") : [MyStruct](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") }`
```
{ [α](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), [β](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference"), [a](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := 10, [b](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example") := [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference") } : [MyStruct](Structures-and-Records/#MyStruct "Definition of example")
```
``
_Record update_ is another common operation which amounts to creating a new record object by modifying the value of one or more fields in an old one. Lean allows you to specify that unassigned fields in the specification of a record should be taken from a previously defined structure object `s` by adding the annotation `s ``with` before the field assignments. If more than one record object is provided, then they are visited in order until Lean finds one that contains the unspecified field. Lean raises an error if any of the field names remain unspecified after all the objects are visited.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where   x : α   y : α deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")  ``def p : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   { x := 1, y := 2 }  ```{ x := 1, y := 3 }`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") { p with y := 3 }`
```
{ x := 1, y := 3 }
```
` ```{ x := 4, y := 2 }`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") { p with x := 4 }`
```
{ x := 4, y := 2 }
```
` ``structure Point3 (α : Type u) where   x : α   y : α   z : α  ``def q : [Point3](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   { [x](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") := 5, [y](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") := 5, [z](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") := 5 }  ``def r : [Point3](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   { p, q with [x](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") := 6 }  ``example : [r](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example").[x](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") = 6 := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") ``example : [r](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example").[y](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") = 2 := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") ``example : [r](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example").[z](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next "Definition of example") = 5 := rfl ```
##  9.3. Inheritance[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=inheritance "Permalink")
We can _extend_ existing structures by adding new fields. This feature allows us to simulate a form of _inheritance_.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where   x : α   y : α  ``inductive Color where   | red | green | blue  ``structure ColorPoint (α : Type u) extends Point α where   c : Color ```
In the next example, we define a structure using multiple inheritance, and then define an object using objects of the parent structures.
`structure Point (α : Type u) where   x : α   y : α   z : α  ``structure RGBValue where   red : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   green : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   blue : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")  ``structure RedGreenPoint (α : Type u) extends Point α, [RGBValue](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example") where   no_blue : blue = 0  ``def p : Point [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   { x := 10, y := 10, [z](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example") := 20 }  ``def rgp : [RedGreenPoint](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   { p with [red](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example") := 200, [green](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example") := 40, [blue](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example") := 0, [no_blue](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example") := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") }  ``example : [rgp](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example").x   = 10 := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") ``example : [rgp](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example").[red](Structures-and-Records/#Point-next-next-next-next-next "Definition of example") = 200 := rfl ```
[←8. Induction and Recursion](Induction-and-Recursion/#induction-and-recursion "8. Induction and Recursion")[10. Type Classes→](Type-Classes/#type-classes "10. Type Classes")

title: Tactics
markdown:
[←4. Quantifiers and Equality](Quantifiers-and-Equality/#quantifiers-and-equality "4. Quantifiers and Equality")[6. Interacting with Lean→](Interacting-with-Lean/#Theorem-Proving-in-Lean-4--Interacting-with-Lean "6. Interacting with Lean")
#  5. Tactics
In this chapter, we describe an alternative approach to constructing proofs, using _tactics_. A proof term is a representation of a mathematical proof; tactics are commands, or instructions, that describe how to build such a proof. Informally, you might begin a mathematical proof by saying “to prove the forward direction, unfold the definition, apply the previous lemma, and simplify.” Just as these are instructions that tell the reader how to find the relevant proof, tactics are instructions that tell Lean how to construct a proof term. They naturally support an incremental style of writing proofs, in which you decompose a proof and work on goals one step at a time.
We will describe proofs that consist of sequences of tactics as “tactic-style” proofs, to contrast with the ways of writing proof terms we have seen so far, which we will call “term-style” proofs. Each style has its own advantages and disadvantages. For example, tactic-style proofs can be harder to read, because they require the reader to predict or guess the results of each instruction. But they can also be shorter and easier to write. Moreover, tactics offer a gateway to using Lean's automation, since automated procedures are themselves tactics.
##  5.1. Entering Tactic Mode[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=entering-tactic-mode "Permalink")
Conceptually, stating a theorem or introducing a `have` statement creates a goal, namely, the goal of constructing a term with the expected type. For example, the following creates the goal of constructing a term of type `p ∧ q ∧ p`, in a context with constants `p q : Prop`, `hp : p` and `hq : q`:
`theorem `declaration uses 'sorry'`test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p [sorry](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#sorry "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 ```
You can write this goal as follows:
p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p
Indeed, if you replace the “sorry” by an underscore in the example above, Lean will report that it is exactly this goal that has been left unsolved.
Ordinarily, you meet such a goal by writing an explicit term. But wherever a term is expected, Lean allows us to insert instead a `by <tactics>` block, where `<tactics>` is a sequence of commands, separated by semicolons or line breaks. You can prove the theorem above in that way:
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p :=   byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ prightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p      [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hprightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p      [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")right.leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ qright.rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p      [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hqright.rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p      [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙 ```
We often put the `by` keyword on the preceding line, and write the example above as:
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ prightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hprightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")right.leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ qright.rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hqright.rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙 ```
The `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic applies an expression, viewed as denoting a function with zero or more arguments. It unifies the conclusion with the expression in the current goal, and creates new goals for the remaining arguments, provided that no later arguments depend on them. In the example above, the command `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")` yields two subgoals:
leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ prightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p
The first goal is met with the command `[exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hp`. The `[exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` command is just a variant of `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` which signals that the expression given should fill the goal exactly. It is good form to use it in a tactic proof, since its failure signals that something has gone wrong. It is also more robust than `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, since the elaborator takes the expected type, given by the target of the goal, into account when processing the expression that is being applied. In this case, however, `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` would work just as well.
You can see the resulting proof term with the `#print` command:
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ prightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hprightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")right.leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ qright.rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hqright.rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙 `
``theorem test : ∀ (p q : Prop), p → q → p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p := fun p q hp hq => [⟨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")hp[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") [⟨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")hq[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hp[⟩](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")[⟩](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")`#print test`

You can write a tactic script incrementally. In VS Code, you can open a window to display messages by pressing ``Ctrl``Shift``Enter``, and that window will then show you the current goal whenever the cursor is in a tactic block. If the proof is incomplete, the token `by` is decorated with a red squiggly line, and the error message contains the remaining goals.
Tactic commands can take compound expressions, not just single identifiers. The following is a shorter version of the preceding proof:
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hpp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hq hpAll goals completed! 🐙 ```
Unsurprisingly, it produces exactly the same proof term:
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p  [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hpp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p  [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hq hpAll goals completed! 🐙 `
``theorem test : ∀ (p q : Prop), p → q → p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p := fun p q hp hq => [⟨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")hp[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") [⟨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")hq[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hp[⟩](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")[⟩](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")`#print test`

``
Multiple tactic applications can be written in a single line by concatenating with a semicolon.
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hpp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p; [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") hq hpAll goals completed! 🐙 ```
Tactics that may produce multiple subgoals often tag them. For example, the tactic `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")` tagged the first subgoal as `leftleftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p`, and the second as `rightrightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p`. In the case of the `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic, the tags are inferred from the parameters' names used in the `[And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")` declaration. You can structure your tactics using the notation `case`` <tag> => <tactics>`. The following is a structured version of our first tactic proof in this chapter.
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ prightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") left =>p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙   [case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") right =>p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p     [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ qrightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p     [case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") left =>p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hqAll goals completed! 🐙     [case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") right =>p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙 ```
You can solve the subgoal `rightrightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p` before `leftleftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p` using the `[case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` notation:
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ prightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") right =>p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p     [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ qrightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p     [case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") left =>p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hqAll goals completed! 🐙     [case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") right =>p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙   [case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") left =>p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙 ```
Note that Lean hides the other goals inside the `[case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` block. After `[case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") left =>`, the proof state is:
p:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q
We say that `[case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` is “focusing” on the selected goal. Moreover, Lean flags an error if the selected goal is not fully solved at the end of the `[case](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#case "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` block.
For simple subgoals, it may not be worth selecting a subgoal using its tag, but you may still want to structure the proof. Lean also provides the “bullet” notation `. <tactics>` (or `· <tactics>`) for structuring proofs:
`theorem test (p q : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ q ∧ p := byp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ prightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   .leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙   .rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")right.leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ qright.rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p     .right.leftp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ q [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hqAll goals completed! 🐙     .right.rightp:Propq:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hpAll goals completed! 🐙 ```
##  5.2. Basic Tactics[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=basic-tactics "Permalink")
In addition to `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` and `[exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, another useful tactic is `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, which introduces a hypothesis. What follows is an example of an identity from propositional logic that we proved in a previous chapter, now proved using tactics.
`example (p q r : Prop) : p ∧ (q ∨ r) ↔ (p ∧ q) ∨ (p ∧ r) := byp:Propq:Propr:Prop⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") [(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference")q [∨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference") r[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference") [↔](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Iff___intro "Documentation for Iff in Lean Language Reference") p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [∨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference") p The `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` command can more generally be used to introduce a variable of any type:
`example (α : Type) : α → α := byα:Type⊢ α → α   [intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") aα:Typea:α⊢ α   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") aAll goals completed! 🐙  ``example (α : Type) : ∀ x : α, x = x := byα:Type⊢ ∀ (x : α), x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x   [intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") xα:Typex:α⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x   [exact](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#exact "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [Eq.refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq.refl in Lean Language Reference") xAll goals completed! 🐙 ```

As the `[apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic is a command for constructing function applications interactively, the `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic is a command for constructing function abstractions interactively (i.e., terms of the form `fun x => e`). As with lambda abstraction notation, the `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic allows us to use an implicit `match`.

The `[intros](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intros "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic can be used without any arguments, in which case, it chooses names and introduces as many variables as it can. You will see an example of this in a moment.
The `[assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic looks through the assumptions in context of the current goal, and if there is one matching the conclusion, it applies it.

Note that names automatically generated by Lean are inaccessible by default. The motivation is to ensure your tactic proofs do not rely on automatically generated names, and are consequently more robust. However, you can use the combinator `[unhygienic](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#unhygienic "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` to disable this restriction.

The `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rfl "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic solves goals that are reflexive relations applied to definitionally equal arguments. Equality is reflexive:
`example (y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : (fun `unused variable `x`  Note: This linter can be disabled with `set_option linter.unusedVariables false``x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => 0) y = 0 := byy:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ (fun x => 0) y [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0 [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rfl "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 ```

Another tactic that is sometimes useful is the `[revert](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#revert "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic, which is, in a sense, an inverse to `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`:
`example (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : x = x := byx:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x   [revert](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#revert "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") x⊢ ∀ (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")), x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x   [intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") yy:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ y [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y   [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rfl "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 ```
After `[revert](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#revert "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") x`, the proof state is:
⊢ ∀ (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")), x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x
After `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") y`, it is:
y:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ y [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y
Moving a hypothesis into the goal yields an implication:
`example (x y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (h : x = y) : y = x := byx:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")y:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h:x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y⊢ y Reference")hx:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")y:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h₁:x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y   [assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 ```
After `[revert](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#revert "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") h`, the proof state is:
x:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")y:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y → y [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x
After `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") h₁`, it is:
x:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")y:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h₁:x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y⊢ y [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x
But `[revert](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#revert "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` is even more clever, in that it will revert not only an element of the context but also all the subsequent elements of the context that depend on it. For example, reverting `x` in the example above brings `h` along with it:
`example (x y : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (h : x = y) : y = x := byx:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")y:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h:x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y⊢ y [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x✝
