As with `rw`, you can send `simp` a list of facts to use, including general lemmas, local hypotheses, definitions to unfold, and compound expressions. The `simp` tactic also recognizes the `←t` syntax that `rewrite` does. In any case, the additional rules are added to the collection of identities that are used to simplify a term.
`def f (m n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   m + n + m  ``example {m n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")} (h : n = 1) (h' : 0 = m) : (f m n) = n := bym:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")n:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h:n [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 1h':0 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") m⊢ f m n [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") n   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [h, ←h', f]All goals completed! 🐙 ```
A common idiom is to simplify a goal using local hypotheses:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (k : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (f : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))  ``example (h₁ : f 0 = 0) (h₂ : k = 0) : f k = 0 := byk:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")f:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h₁:f 0 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0h₂:k [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0⊢ f k [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [h₁, h₂]All goals completed! 🐙 ```
To use all the hypotheses present in the local context when simplifying, we can use the wildcard symbol, `*`:
`[variable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___variable "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (k : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (f : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))  ``example (h₁ : f 0 = 0) (h₂ : k = 0) : f k = 0 := byk:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")f:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")h₁:f 0 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0h₂:k [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0⊢ f k [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [*]All goals completed! 🐙 ```

The simplifier will also do propositional rewriting. For example, using the hypothesis `p`, it rewrites `p ∧ q` to `q` and `p ∨ q` to `[True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference")`, which it then proves trivially. Iterating such rewrites produces nontrivial propositional reasoning.
`example (p q : Prop) (hp : p) : p ∧ q ↔ q := byp:Propq:Prophp:p⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") q [↔](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Iff___intro "Documentation for Iff in Lean Language Reference") q   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [*]All goals completed! 🐙  ``example (p q : Prop) (hp : p) : p ∨ q := byp:Propq:Prophp:p⊢ p [∨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference") q   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [*]All goals completed! 🐙  ``example (p q r : Prop) (hp : p) (hq : q) : p ∧ (q ∨ r) := byp:Propq:Propr:Prophp:phq:q⊢ p [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") [(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference")q [∨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference") r[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference")   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [*]All goals completed! 🐙 ```

One thing that makes the simplifier especially useful is that its capabilities can grow as a library develops. For example, suppose we define a list operation that symmetrizes its input by appending its reversal:
`def mk_symm (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) :=   xs ++ xs.[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference") ```
Then for any list `xs`, `(mk_symm xs).[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference")` is equal to `mk_symm xs`, which can easily be proved by unfolding the definition:
`def mk_symm (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) :=  xs ++ xs.[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference") `
`theorem reverse_mk_symm (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α)         : (mk_symm xs).[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference") = mk_symm xs := byα:Type u_1xs:[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α⊢ (mk_symm xs).[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference") [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") mk_symm xs   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [mk_symm]All goals completed! 🐙 ```

Once the attribute is applied, however, there is no way to permanently remove it; it persists in any file that imports the one where the attribute is assigned. As we will discuss further in [Attributes](Interacting-with-Lean/#attributes), one can limit the scope of an attribute to the current file or section using the `local` modifier:

Outside the section, the simplifier will no longer use `reverse_mk_symm` by default.
Note that the various `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` options we have discussed—giving an explicit list of rules, and using `at` to specify the location—can be combined, but the order they are listed is rigid. You can see the correct order in an editor by placing the cursor on the `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` identifier to see the documentation string that is associated with it.
There are two additional modifiers that are useful. By default, `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` includes all theorems that have been marked with the attribute `[simp]`. Writing `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") only` excludes these defaults, allowing you to use a more explicitly crafted list of rules. In the examples below, the minus sign and `only` are used to block the application of `reverse_mk_symm`.

The `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic has many configuration options. For example, we can enable contextual simplifications as follows:
`example : [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x = 0 [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") y + x = y [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x ≠ 0 := byx:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")y:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ if x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0 then y [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y else x ≠ 0   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") +[contextual](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Simplifier/Configuring-Simplification/#Lean___Meta___Simp___Config___mk "Documentation for Lean.Meta.Simp.Config.contextual in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 ```
With `+[contextual](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Simplifier/Configuring-Simplification/#Lean___Meta___Simp___Config___mk "Documentation for Lean.Meta.Simp.Config.contextual in Lean Language Reference")`, the `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic uses the fact that `x = 0` when simplifying `y + x = y`, and `x ≠ 0` when simplifying the other branch. Here is another example:
`example : ∀ (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (`unused variable `h`  Note: This linter can be disabled with `set_option linter.unusedVariables false``h : x = 0), y + x = y := byy:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ ∀ (x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")), x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 0 → y [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") y [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") +[contextual](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Simplifier/Configuring-Simplification/#Lean___Meta___Simp___Config___mk "Documentation for Lean.Meta.Simp.Config.contextual in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 ```
Another useful configuration option is `+[arith](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Simplifier/Configuring-Simplification/#Lean___Meta___Simp___Config___mk "Documentation for Lean.Meta.Simp.Config.arith in Lean Language Reference")` which enables arithmetical simplifications.

##  5.8. Split Tactic[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=split-tactic "Permalink")
The `[split](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#split "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic is useful for breaking nested `if`-`then`-`else` and `match` expressions in cases. For a `match` expression with `nnn` cases, the `[split](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#split "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic generates at most `nnn` subgoals. Here is an example:

[match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x, y, z The tactic `[split](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#split "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") <;> [first](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#first "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") | [contradiction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#contradiction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") | [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rfl "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` first applies the `[split](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#split "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic, and then for each generated goal it tries `[contradiction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#contradiction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, and then `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rfl "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` if `[contradiction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#contradiction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` fails. Like `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, we can apply `[split](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#split "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` to a particular hypothesis:
`def g (xs ys : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs, ys [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [a, b], _ => a+b+1   | _, [b, _] => ##  5.9. Extensible Tactics[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=extensible-tactics "Permalink")
In the following example, we define the notation `triv` using the command `[syntax](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Notations-and-Macros/Defining-New-Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Command___syntax "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")`. Then, we use the command `[macro_rules](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Notations-and-Macros/Macros/#Lean___Parser___Command___macro_rules "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` to specify what should be done when `triv` is used. You can provide different expansions, and the tactic interpreter will try all of them until one succeeds:
`-- Define a new tactic notation [syntax](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Notations-and-Macros/Defining-New-Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Command___syntax "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "triv" : tactic  ``[macro_rules](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Notations-and-Macros/Macros/#Lean___Parser___Command___macro_rules "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | `(tactic| triv) => `(tactic| [assumption](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#assumption "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference"))  ``example (h : p) : p := byp:Sort ?u.2129h:p⊢ p   trivAll goals completed! 🐙  ``-- You cannot prove the following theorem using `triv` -- example (x : α) : x = x := by --  triv  -- Let's extend `triv`. The tactic interpreter -- tries all possible macro extensions for `triv` until one succeeds [macro_rules](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Notations-and-Macros/Macros/#Lean___Parser___Command___macro_rules "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | `(tactic| triv) => `(tactic| [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rfl "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference"))  ``example (x : α) : x = x := byα:Sort u_1x:α⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x   trivAll goals completed! 🐙  ``example (x : α) (h : p) : x = x ∧ p := byα:Sort u_1p:Propx:αh:p⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")leftα:Sort u_1p:Propx:αh:p⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") xrightα:Sort u_1p:Propx:αh:p⊢ p <;>leftα:Sort u_1p:Propx:αh:p⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") xrightα:Sort u_1p:Propx:αh:p⊢ p trivAll goals completed! 🐙  ``-- We now add a (recursive) extension [macro_rules](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Notations-and-Macros/Macros/#Lean___Parser___Command___macro_rules "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") | `(tactic| triv) => `(tactic| [apply](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#apply "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") And.intro <;> triv)  ``example (x : α) (h : p) : x = x ∧ p := byα:Sort u_1p:Propx:αh:p⊢ x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") p   trivAll goals completed! 🐙 ```
##  5.10. Exercises
  1. Go back to the exercises in [Propositions and Proofs](Propositions-and-Proofs/#propositions-and-proofs) and [Quantifiers and Equality](Quantifiers-and-Equality/#quantifiers-and-equality) and redo as many as you can now with tactic proofs, using also `rw` and `simp` as appropriate.
  2. Use tactic combinators to obtain a one-line proof of the following:

title: The Conversion Tactic Mode
markdown:
[←10. Type Classes](Type-Classes/#type-classes "10. Type Classes")[12. Axioms and Computation→](Axioms-and-Computation/#axioms-and-computation "12. Axioms and Computation")
#  11. The Conversion Tactic Mode[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=conv "Permalink")
Inside a tactic block, one can use the keyword `conv` to enter _conversion mode_. This mode allows to travel inside assumptions and goals, even inside function abstractions and dependent arrows, to apply rewriting or simplifying steps.
##  11.1. Basic navigation and rewriting[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=basic-navigation-and-rewriting "Permalink")
As a first example, let us prove example `(a b c : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : a * (b * c) = a * (c * b)` (examples in this file are somewhat artificial since other tactics could finish them immediately). The naive first attempt is to enter tactic mode and try `[rw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rw "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [Nat.mul_comm]`. But this transforms the goal into `b [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") c [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") a [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") a [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") [(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference")c [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") b[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference")`, after commuting the very first multiplication appearing in the term. There are several ways to fix this issue, and one way is to use a more precise tool: the conversion mode. The following code block shows the current target after each line.

[Nat.mul_comm]aa:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")b:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")c:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")| c [*](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") b ```
The above snippet shows three navigation commands:
  * `lhs` navigates to the left-hand side of a relation (equality, in this case). There is also a `rhs` to navigate to the right-hand side.
  * `congr` creates as many targets as there are (nondependent and explicit) arguments to the current head function (here the head function is multiplication).
  * `rfl` closes target using reflexivity.


Once arrived at the relevant target, we can use `[rw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rw "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` as in normal tactic mode.
The second main reason to use conversion mode is to rewrite under binders. Suppose we want to prove example `(fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => 0 + x) = (fun x => x)`. The naive first attempt is to enter tactic mode and try `rw [Nat.zero_add]`. But this fails with a frustrating

```
error: tactic 'rewrite' failed, did not find instance of the pattern
       in the target expression
  0 + ?n
⊢ (fun x => 0 + x) = fun x => x

```

The solution is:
`example : (fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => 0 + x) = (fun x => x) :=  by⊢ (fun x => 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x) [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") fun x => x   conv =>| (fun x => 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x) [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") fun x => x     lhs| fun x => 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x     intro xhx:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")| 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x     rw [Nat.zero_add]hx:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")| x ```
where `intro x` is the navigation command entering inside the `fun` binder. Note that this example is somewhat artificial, one could also do:
`example : (fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => 0 + x) = (fun x => x) := by⊢ (fun x => 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x) [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") fun x => x   [funext](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#funext-next "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") xhx:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") x; [rw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#rw "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [Nat.zero_add]All goals completed! 🐙 ```
or just
`example : (fun x : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") => 0 + x) = (fun x => x) := by⊢ (fun x => 0 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x) [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") fun x => x   [simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 ```
`conv` can also rewrite a hypothesis `h` from the local context, using `conv at`` h`.
##  11.2. Pattern matching[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=pattern-matching-conv "Permalink")

  * `enter`` [1, x, 2, y]` iterate `arg` and `intro` with the given arguments.
  * `done` fail if there are unsolved goals.
  * `trace_state` display the current tactic state.
  * `whnf` put term in weak head normal form.
  * `tactic`` => <tactic sequence>` go back to regular tactic mode. This is useful for discharging goals not supported by `conv` mode, and applying custom congruence and extensionality lemmas.

title: Type Classes
markdown:
[←9. Structures and Records](Structures-and-Records/#structures-and-records "9. Structures and Records")[11. The Conversion Tactic Mode→](The-Conversion-Tactic-Mode/#conv "11. The Conversion Tactic Mode")
#  10. Type Classes[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=type-classes "Permalink")
Type classes were introduced as a principled way of enabling ad-hoc polymorphism in functional programming languages. We first observe that it would be easy to implement an ad-hoc polymorphic function (such as addition) if the function simply took the type-specific implementation of addition as an argument and then called that implementation on the remaining arguments. For example, suppose we declare a structure in Lean to hold implementations of addition.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex `
`structure Add (α : Type) where   add : α → α → α  ```@Add.add : {α : Type} → Add α → α → α → α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @Add.add`
```
@Add.add : {α : Type} → Add α → α → α → α
```

`end Ex `
In the above Lean code, the field `add` has type `Add.add : {α : Type} → Add α → α → α → α` where the curly braces around the type `α` mean that it is an implicit argument. We could implement `double` by:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex structure Add (α : Type) where   add : α → α → α `
`def double (s : Add α) (x : α) : α :=   s.add x x  ```20`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double { add := [Nat.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___add "Documentation for Nat.add in Lean Language Reference") } 10`
```
20
```
` ```100`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double { add := [Nat.mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___mul "Documentation for Nat.mul in Lean Language Reference") } 10`
```
100
```
` ```20`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double { add := [Int.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___add "Documentation for Int.add in Lean Language Reference") } 10`
```
20
```

`end Ex `
Note that you can double a natural number `n` by `double { add := [Nat.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___add "Documentation for Nat.add in Lean Language Reference") } n`. Of course, it would be highly cumbersome for users to manually pass the implementations around in this way. Indeed, it would defeat most of the potential benefits of ad-hoc polymorphism.
The main idea behind type classes is to make arguments such as `Add α` implicit, and to use a database of user-defined instances to synthesize the desired instances automatically through a process known as typeclass resolution. In Lean, by changing `structure` to `class` in the example above, the type of `Add.add` becomes:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex `
`class Add (α : Type) where   add : α → α → α  ```@Add.add : {α : Type} → [self : Add α] → α → α → α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @Add.add`
```
@Add.add : {α : Type} → [self : Add α] → α → α → α
```

`end Ex `
where the square brackets indicate that the argument of type `Add α` is _instance implicit_ , i.e. that it should be synthesized using typeclass resolution. This version of `add` is the Lean analogue of the Haskell term `add :: Add a => a -> a -> a`. Similarly, we can register instances by:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex class Add (α : Type) where   add : α → α → α `
`instance : Add [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where   add := [Nat.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___add "Documentation for Nat.add in Lean Language Reference")  ``instance : Add [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") where   add := [Int.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___add "Documentation for Int.add in Lean Language Reference")  ``instance : Add [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference") where   add := [Float.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float___add "Documentation for Float.add in Lean Language Reference") `
`end Ex `
Then for `n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` and `m : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, the term `Add.add n m` triggers typeclass resolution with the goal of `Add [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, and typeclass resolution will synthesize the instance for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` above. We can now reimplement `double` using an instance implicit by:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex class Add (α : Type) where   add : α → α → α instance : Add [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where  add := [Nat.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___add "Documentation for Nat.add in Lean Language Reference") instance : Add [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") where  add := [Int.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___add "Documentation for Int.add in Lean Language Reference") instance : Add [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference") where  add := [Float.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float___add "Documentation for Float.add in Lean Language Reference") `
`def double [Add α] (x : α) : α :=   Add.add x x  ```@double : {α : Type} → [Add α] → α → α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @double`
```
@double : {α : Type} → [Add α] → α → α
```
` ```20`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double 10`
```
20
```
` ```20`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double (10 : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
20
```
` ```14.000000`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double (7 : [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
14.000000
```
` ```482.000000`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") double (239.0 + 2)`
```
482.000000
```
` `
`end Ex `
In general, instances may depend on other instances in complicated ways. For example, you can declare an instance stating that if `α` has addition, then `[Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α` has addition:
`instance [[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference") α] : [Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference") ([Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α) where   [add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add.add in Lean Language Reference") x y := [Array.zipWith](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___zipWith "Documentation for Array.zipWith in Lean Language Reference") (· + ·) x y  ```#[4, 6]`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Add.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add.add in Lean Language Reference") #[1, 2] #[3, 4]`
```
#[4, 6]
```
` ```#[4, 6]`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") #[1, 2] + #[3, 4]`
```
#[4, 6]
```
``
Note that `(· + ·)` is notation for `fun x y => x + y` in Lean.
The example above demonstrates how type classes are used to overload notation. Now, we explore another application. We often need an arbitrary element of a given type. Recall that types may not have any elements in Lean. It often happens that we would like a definition to return an arbitrary element in a “corner case.” For example, we may like the expression `head xs` to be of type `α` when `xs` is of type `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`. Similarly, many theorems hold under the additional assumption that a type is not empty. For example, if `α` is a type, `∃ x : α, x = x` is true only if `α` is not empty. The standard library defines a type class `[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference")` to enable type class inference to infer a “default” element of an inhabited type. Let us start with the first step of the program above, declaring an appropriate class:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex `
`class Inhabited (α : Type u) where   default : α  ```[@](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example")[Inhabited.default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") : {α : Type u_1} → [self : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") α] → α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") @Inhabited.default`
```
[@](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example")[Inhabited.default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") : {α : Type u_1} → [self : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") α] → α
```

`end Ex `
Note `Inhabited.default` doesn't have any explicit arguments.
An element of the class `[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") α` is simply an expression of the form `[Inhabited.mk](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.mk in Lean Language Reference") x`, for some element `x : α`. The projection `[Inhabited.default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference")` will allow us to “extract” such an element of `α` from an element of `[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") α`. Now we populate the class with some instances:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex class Inhabited (a : Type _) where  default : a `
`instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") where   [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")  ``instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where   [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := 0  ``instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") where   [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := ()  ``instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") Prop where   [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference")  ```0`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([Inhabited.default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
0
```
` ```true`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([Inhabited.default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
true
```

`end Ex `
You can use the command `export` to create the alias `[default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference")` for `[Inhabited.default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference")`.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex class Inhabited (a : Type _) where  default : a instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") where  [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference") instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where  [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := 0 instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") where  [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := () instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") Prop where  [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference") `
`[export](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___export "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Inhabited ([default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example"))  ```0`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```

##  10.1. Chaining Instances[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=chaining-instances "Permalink")
If that were the extent of type class inference, it would not be all that impressive; it would be simply a mechanism of storing a list of instances for the elaborator to find in a lookup table. What makes type class inference powerful is that one can _chain_ instances. That is, an instance declaration can in turn depend on an implicit instance of a type class. This causes class inference to chain through instances recursively, backtracking when necessary, in a Prolog-like search.
For example, the following definition shows that if two types `α` and `β` are inhabited, then so is their product:
`instance [[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") α] [[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") β] : [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") (α × β) where   [default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference") := ([default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference"), [default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference")) ```
With this added to the earlier instance declarations, type class instance can infer, for example, a default element of `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`:
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex class Inhabited (α : Type u) where  default : α instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") where  [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference") instance : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where  [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := 0 opaque default [[Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") α] : α :=  [Inhabited.default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") `
`instance [[Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") α] [[Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") β] : [Inhabited](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") (α × β) where   [default](Type-Classes/#Ex___Inhabited "Definition of example") := (default, default)  ```(0, true)`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (default : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
(0, true)
```

`end Ex `
Similarly, we can inhabit type function with suitable constant functions:
`instance [[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") β] : [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") (α → β) where   [default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference") := fun _ => [default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference") ```
As an exercise, try defining default instances for other types, such as `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Sum](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum in Lean Language Reference")` types.
The Lean standard library contains the definition `[inferInstance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#inferInstance "Documentation for inferInstance in Lean Language Reference")`. It has type `{α : Sort u} → [i : α] → α`, and is useful for triggering the type class resolution procedure when the expected type is an instance.
``[inferInstance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#inferInstance "Documentation for inferInstance in Lean Language Reference") : [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([inferInstance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#inferInstance "Documentation for inferInstance in Lean Language Reference") : [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
[inferInstance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#inferInstance "Documentation for inferInstance in Lean Language Reference") : [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
` ``def foo : [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) :=   [inferInstance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#inferInstance "Documentation for inferInstance in Lean Language Reference")  ``theorem ex : foo.[default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference") = ([default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference"), [default](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited.default in Lean Language Reference")) :=   rfl ```
You can use the command `#print` to inspect how simple `inferInstance` is.
``@[reducible] def inferInstance.{u} : {α : Sort u} → [i : α] → α := fun {α} [i : α] => i`#print inferInstance`
```
@[reducible] def inferInstance.{u} : {α : Sort u} → [i : α] → α :=
fun {α} [i : α] => i
```
``
##  10.2. ToString[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=ToString "Permalink")
The polymorphic method `toString` has type `{α : Type u} → [ToString α] → α → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`. You implement the instance for your own types and use chaining to convert complex values into strings. Lean comes with `ToString` instances for most builtin types.
`structure Person where   name : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___ofByteArray "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")   age  : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")  ``instance : ToString [Person](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example") where   toString p := p.[name](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example") ++ "@" ++ toString p.[age](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example")  ```"Leo@542"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") toString { [name](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example") := "Leo", [age](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example") := 542 : [Person](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example") }`
```
"Leo@542"
```
` ```"(Daniel@18, hello)"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") toString ({ [name](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example") := "Daniel", [age](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example") := 18 : [Person](Type-Classes/#Person "Definition of example") }, "hello")`
```
"(Daniel@18, hello)"
```
``
##  10.3. Numerals[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=numerals "Permalink")
Numerals are polymorphic in Lean. You can use a numeral (e.g., `2`) to denote an element of any type that implements the type class `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Numeric-Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")`.
`structure Rational where   num : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")   den : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   inv : den ≠ 0  ``instance : [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Numeric-Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") Rational n where   [ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Numeric-Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") := { num := n, den := 1, inv := byn:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ 1 ≠ 0 [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 }  ``instance : ToString Rational where   toString r := s!"{r.num}/{r.den}"  ```2/1`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (2 : Rational)`
```
2/1
```
` ```2 : Rational`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (2 : Rational)`
```
2 : Rational
```
` ```2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``
Lean elaborates the terms `(2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))` and `(2 : Rational)` as `@[OfNat.ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Numeric-Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") 2 (@instOfNatNat 2)` and `@[OfNat.ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Numeric-Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") Rational 2 (@instOfNatRational 2)` respectively. We say the numerals `2` occurring in the elaborated terms are _raw_ natural numbers. You can input the raw natural number `2` using the macro `nat_lit 2`.
``2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") nat_lit 2`
```
2 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```
``
Raw natural numbers are _not_ polymorphic.
The `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Numeric-Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")` instance is parametric on the numeral. So, you can define instances for particular numerals. The second argument is often a variable as in the example above, or a _raw_ natural number.
`class Monoid (α : Type u) where   unit : α   op   : α → α → α  ``instance [s : [Monoid](Type-Classes/#Monoid "Definition of example") α] : [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Numeric-Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") α (nat_lit 1) where   [ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Numeric-Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") := s.[unit](Type-Classes/#Monoid "Definition of example")  ``def getUnit [[Monoid](Type-Classes/#Monoid "Definition of example") α] : α :=   1 ```
##  10.4. Output Parameters[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=output-parameters "Permalink")
By default, Lean only tries to synthesize an instance `[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") T` when the term `T` is known and does not contain missing parts. The following command produces the error `typeclass instance problem is stuck, it is often due to metavariables` because the type has a missing part (i.e., the `_`).
`/-- error: typeclass instance problem is stuck, it is often due to metavariables   Inhabited (Nat × ?m.2) -/ [#guard_msgs](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___guardMsgsCmd "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (error) [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___guardMsgsCmd "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([inferInstance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#inferInstance "Documentation for inferInstance in Lean Language Reference") : [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × _)) ```
You can view the parameter of the type class `[Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference")` as an _input_ value for the type class synthesizer. When a type class has multiple parameters, you can mark some of them as _output parameters_. Lean will start type class synthesizer even when these parameters have missing parts. In the following example, we use output parameters to define a _heterogeneous_ polymorphic multiplication.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex `
`class HMul (α : Type u) (β : Type v) (γ : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (Type w)) where   hMul : α → β → γ  ``[export](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___export "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") HMul (hMul)  ``instance : HMul [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where   hMul := [Nat.mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___mul "Documentation for Nat.mul in Lean Language Reference")  ``instance : HMul [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") ([Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) ([Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) where   hMul a bs := bs.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___map "Documentation for Array.map in Lean Language Reference") (fun b => hMul a b)  ```12`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") hMul 4 3`
```

```
` ```#[8, 12, 16]`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") hMul 4 #[2, 3, 4]`
```
#[8, 12, 16]
```

`end Ex `
The parameters `α` and `β` are considered input parameters and `γ` an output one. Given an application `hMul a b`, after the types of `a` and `b` are known, the type class synthesizer is invoked, and the resulting type is obtained from the output parameter `γ`. In the example above, we defined two instances. The first one is the homogeneous multiplication for natural numbers. The second is the scalar multiplication for arrays. Note that you chain instances and generalize the second instance.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex `
`class HMul (α : Type u) (β : Type v) (γ : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") [Int.mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___mul "Documentation for Int.mul in Lean Language Reference")  ``instance [HMul α β γ] : HMul α bs.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___map "Documentation for Array.map in Lean Language Reference") (fun b => hMul a b)  ```12`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") hMul 4 3`
```

```

```
` ```#[-6, 2, -8]`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") hMul (-2) #[3, -1, 4]`
```

You can use our new scalar array multiplication instance on arrays of type `[Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") β` with a scalar of type `α` whenever you have an instance `HMul α β γ`. In the last `#eval`, note that the instance was used twice on an array of arrays.
Output parameters are ignored during instance synthesis. Even when instance synthesis occurs in a context in which the values of output parameters are already determined, their values are ignored. Once an instance is found using its input parameters, Lean ensures that the already-known values of the output parameters match those which were found.
Lean also features _semi-output parameters_ , which have some features of input parameters and some features of output parameters. Like input parameters, semi-output parameters are considered when selecting instances. Like output parameters, they can be used to instantiate unknown values. However, they do not do so uniquely. Instance synthesis with semi-output parameters can be more difficult to predict, because the order in which instances are considered can determine which is selected, but it is also more flexible.
##  10.5. Default Instances[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=default-instances "Permalink")
In the class `HMul`, the parameters `α` and `β` are treated as input values. Thus, type class synthesis only starts after these two types are known. This may often be too restrictive.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex `
`class HMul (α : Type u) (β : Type v) (γ : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (Type w)) where   hMul : α → β → γ  ``[export](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___export "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") HMul (hMul)  ``instance : HMul [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") where   hMul := [Int.mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___mul "Documentation for Int.mul in Lean Language Reference")  ``def xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") := [1, 2, 3]  ``/-- error: typeclass instance problem is stuck   HMul Int ?m.2 (?m.11 y)  Note: Lean will not try to resolve this typeclass instance problem because the second type argument to `HMul` is a metavariable. This argument must be fully determined before Lean will try to resolve the typeclass.  Hint: Adding type annotations and supplying implicit arguments to functions can give Lean more information for typeclass resolution. For example, if you have a variable `x` that you intend to be a `Nat`, but Lean reports it as having an unresolved type like `?m`, replacing `x` with `(x : Nat)` can get typeclass resolution un-stuck. -/ [#guard_msgs](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___guardMsgsCmd "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (error) [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___guardMsgsCmd "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") fun y => xs.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") (fun x => hMul x y) `
`end Ex `
The instance `HMul` is not synthesized by Lean because the type of `y` has not been provided. However, it is natural to assume that the type of `y` and `x` should be the same in this kind of situation. We can achieve exactly that using _default instances_.
`[namespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___namespace "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Ex `
`class HMul (α : Type u) (β : Type v) (γ : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (Type w)) where   hMul : α → β → γ  ``[export](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___export "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") HMul (hMul)  ``@[[default_instance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Declarations/#Lean___Parser___Attr___default_instance "Documentation for syntax in Lean
